report_text: After induction of anesthesia and securing the airway with intubation, the patient was positioned for surgical access. Initial inspection with a laryngoscope revealed a significant ulcerative lesion at the anterior commissure of the glottis, extending to the mid-portion of the right vocal fold. The subglottic space appeared clear. A laser was employed to excise the lesion, maintaining a margin of healthy tissue. The resection was carried out meticulously to preserve the function of the larynx. Hemostasis was achieved with cauterization. Frozen sections confirmed clear margins, confirming an R0 resection. Postoperative care included ensuring airway patency and voice rest.

The patient was placed under general anesthesia with endotracheal intubation. Examination with a fiberoptic scope revealed a large mass occupying the left tonsillar fossa, extending inferiorly into the oropharynx and laterally encroaching on the lateral pharyngeal wall. A transoral approach was taken for excision. The mass was dissected free from surrounding structures with care to preserve the integrity of the pharynx. The resection was extended to the base of the tongue to ensure complete tumor removal. Specimens were sent for frozen section analysis, which confirmed a clear resection margin. Surgical site was closed with absorbable sutures.

General anesthesia was induced and the patient was intubated. A direct laryngoscopy was performed, revealing a fungating mass on the anterior two-thirds of the left vocal cord with extension to the anterior commissure. The right vocal cord was not involved. The tumor was excised using cold steel instruments to allow for precise removal and preservation of the surrounding structures. Hemostasis was achieved using bipolar cautery. Intraoperative frozen sections confirmed the absence of tumor at the margins. The patient was extubated in the operating room and transferred to recovery in stable condition.

Following general anesthesia induction, examination revealed a malignant-appearing lesion on the lateral border of the mid-tongue, extending to the floor of the mouth. The area was exposed using a mouth retractor, and the lesion was excised with a 1 cm margin, including a portion of the floor of the mouth and sublingual gland. The dissection was continued until the lingual nerve, which was carefully preserved. Hemostasis was ensured, and the excised tissue was sent for histopathological assessment. The surgical area was closed primarily, and the patient tolerated the procedure well.

The patient was anesthetized and intubated, and a flexible endoscope was used to visualize the hypopharynx. A large, infiltrative tumor was noted on the posterior pharyngeal wall, extending into the piriform sinus bilaterally and involving the postcricoid region. A transcervical approach was adopted for better access. The tumor was carefully excised, maintaining a safe margin of healthy tissue. The pyriform sinuses were resected bilaterally with care taken to preserve the laryngeal structure. Hemostasis was achieved, and the specimen was sent for pathological evaluation, confirming complete excision.

The patient was brought to the operating room where induction of anesthesia was performed. Following intubation, a direct laryngoscopy was initiated using a 0° endoscope. Examination revealed an ulcerative lesion occupying the right lateral border of the tongue extending towards the base. The lesion appeared to be infiltrative with irregular margins. A transoral laser microsurgery approach was used for precise resection. The tumor was excised with a 1.5 cm margin, including part of the adjacent floor of the mouth. The specimen was sent for frozen section analysis, which confirmed clear margins. Hemostasis was achieved, and a nasogastric tube was placed for feeding postoperatively.

Anesthesia was induced, and the patient was intubated without complications. The surgical team proceeded with a lateral pharyngotomy to access the tumor located at the lateral wall of the oropharynx, extending to the tonsillar region. The mass was exophytic and partially obstructing the oropharyngeal airway. Using electrocautery, resection of the tumor was performed with a 2 cm margin. The tumor was noted to invade the base of the tongue and the surrounding lymphoid tissue. Frozen section analysis confirmed the presence of malignancy with clear surgical margins. Reconstruction with a radial forearm free flap was undertaken to restore the oropharyngeal continuity.

The patient was positioned, and anesthesia was induced with subsequent intubation. A laryngoscopic examination revealed a large, irregular mass involving the left piriform sinus extending into the aryepiglottic fold and partially obstructing the laryngeal inlet. The tumor was resected using a combined external and transoral approach. The external approach involved a lateral neck incision to gain access to the hypopharyngeal structures. The tumor was dissected free from the surrounding tissues, and a wide margin was achieved. Frozen sections confirmed the extent of tumor invasion and guided the resection margins. The surgical site was reconstructed using a free flap.

Following induction of anesthesia, the patient was intubated. An examination with a laryngoscope revealed a bulky tumor on the right vocal cord extending into the anterior commissure and involving the subglottic region. The tumor was exophytic and appeared to compromise vocal cord mobility. A transoral laser excision was performed to remove the tumor, taking care to preserve the contralateral vocal cord. The specimen was sent for frozen section, which confirmed the presence of malignant cells within the resected margins. Additional resection was performed to achieve clear margins, and hemostasis was secured with laser coagulation.

After successful induction of anesthesia, the patient was intubated. The initial inspection with the Kleinsasser tube revealed a large lesion involving the supraglottic larynx. The lesion was infiltrative, extending from the epiglottis to the aryepiglottic folds and down to the false vocal cords. The surgical approach was a supraglottic laryngectomy with resection of the epiglottis, false vocal cords, and part of the aryepiglottic folds. The resected specimen was sent for frozen section analysis, with all margins reported clear. A tracheostomy was performed to secure the airway postoperatively, and the patient was extubated in the operating room.

The patient was brought to the operating room and anesthesia was induced. The mouth was propped open with a retractor, and the tumor was inspected. A large exophytic lesion was noted at the base of the tongue, extending laterally to involve part of the left tonsillar region. Using a monopolar cautery, the lesion was resected with a 1 cm margin. The resection extended to the floor of the mouth on the left side. The lingual nerve was preserved. Hemostasis was achieved, and the specimen was sent for pathologic evaluation. Postoperatively, the patient was extubated without complication and transferred to the recovery room in stable condition.

After induction of anesthesia, the patient was intubated and positioned appropriately. A laryngoscope was inserted, which revealed a large, irregularly shaped mass occupying the entire right piriform sinus. The tumor was infiltrating into the lateral wall of the hypopharynx and extended inferiorly to the esophageal inlet. A transoral laser microsurgery was performed to resect the tumor. Care was taken to preserve the esophageal entry and the contralateral piriform sinus. The resection was completed with clear margins, and all specimens were sent for frozen section analysis, confirming R0 resection. Hemostasis was achieved, and the patient was taken to recovery in stable condition.

Anesthesia was administered, and the patient was intubated. Examination with a laryngoscope revealed a mass on the anterior two-thirds of the left vocal fold and extending into the anterior commissure. The tumor was well-circumscribed, with no invasion of the right vocal fold. Using a CO2 laser, the mass was excised with a 5 mm margin, ensuring preservation of the vocal ligament. The excised specimen was sent for frozen section analysis, confirming negative margins. After ensuring hemostasis, the patient was extubated and transferred to recovery in stable condition.

The patient was anesthetized and intubated. Initial inspection with the Kleinsasser tube showed a tumor originating from the oropharyngeal wall and involving the right side of the soft palate and uvula. The tumor extended anteriorly to the posterior third of the tongue. Resection was initiated with a monopolar cautery, ensuring a 1 cm margin around the tumor. The resection extended to the posterior pharyngeal wall, preserving the contralateral side. Hemostasis was achieved, and the specimen was sent for pathological examination. The patient was extubated and transferred to recovery in stable condition.

After induction of anesthesia, the patient's mouth was opened with a retractor. Examination revealed a large mass in the supraglottic larynx, spanning from the base of the epiglottis to the aryepiglottic fold on the left side. The mass was excised using a CO2 laser, ensuring a 1 cm margin. The resection extended to the false vocal cords, with care taken not to damage the true vocal cords. Hemostasis was achieved, and the specimen was sent for analysis. The patient was extubated and transferred to the recovery area in stable condition.

Anesthesia was induced, and the patient was intubated by the anesthesiology team. A thorough examination was performed using a laryngoscope, revealing a prominent mass on the anterior two-thirds of the right vocal fold, extending into the subglottic region. The mass had infiltrated the anterior commissure but showed no involvement of the posterior glottis. Utilizing laser technology, a precise resection of the mass was performed, ensuring clean margins. During the procedure, it was noted that the right vocal fold exhibited reduced mobility. Hemostasis was achieved using suction and cautery. The resected tissue was sent for pathological analysis, confirming the diagnosis of squamous cell carcinoma. The patient tolerated the procedure well and was extubated under direct visualization.

The patient was placed under general anesthesia, and a mouth retractor was used to gain access to the oral cavity. Examination revealed an extensive lesion on the left lateral border of the tongue, extending towards the base of the tongue. A wide local excision was performed, including a portion of the floor of the mouth, ensuring a margin of at least 1 cm around the visible tumor. The submandibular duct was preserved, but the lingual nerve was partially resected due to tumor involvement. Hemostasis was achieved, and the specimen was sent for frozen section analysis, which confirmed clear margins. A nasogastric tube was placed for postoperative feeding, and the patient was transferred to recovery in stable condition.

Under general anesthesia, the patient was intubated, and the surgical site was prepped and draped. A flexible esophagoscope was introduced to evaluate the extent of the oropharyngeal tumor. A large exophytic mass was identified at the oropharynx, involving the vallecula and extending to the lateral pharyngeal wall. The tumor was excised using a transoral laser microsurgery approach, ensuring complete removal while preserving surrounding structures. The resection included part of the tonsillar region and the base of the tongue. Frozen sections were taken intraoperatively, confirming negative margins. The patient was monitored for bleeding and swelling before being extubated and transferred to the recovery unit.

The surgical team initiated the procedure with intubation and administration of general anesthesia. Examination through the laryngoscope revealed a large, ulcerative mass on the right side of the supraglottic larynx, extending into the aryepiglottic fold and piriform sinus. The mass was primarily localized in the supraglottic region but showed signs of invasion into the laryngeal ventricle. The decision was made to perform a partial laryngectomy with laser resection to preserve vocal function. The tumor was meticulously excised, ensuring adequate margins, and the edges were cauterized. The patient was extubated postoperatively and transferred to the ICU for close monitoring.

After induction of anesthesia, a comprehensive endoscopic assessment was conducted using a rigid endoscope. A suspicious lesion was identified at the base of the tongue, extending into the right tonsillar fossa and infiltrating the soft palate. The lesion was biopsied, confirming malignancy. A wide local excision was performed, including partial resection of the soft palate and tonsillar region, ensuring a 1.5 cm margin around the tumor. The excised tissue was sent for histopathological evaluation, which confirmed squamous cell carcinoma with clear margins. Postoperatively, the patient was observed for airway compromise and bleeding, with no immediate complications noted.

The patient was positioned supine, and anesthesia was induced smoothly. The oral cavity was accessed using a mouth gag, and thorough examination revealed a lesion on the left lateral border of the tongue, approximately 2.5 cm in diameter. The lesion extended posteriorly towards the base of the tongue. Using a scalpel, an incision was made with a 1 cm margin surrounding the lesion. The left lingual nerve was identified and preserved. The resection proceeded caudally, involving partial resection of the floor of the mouth. Hemostasis was achieved using bipolar cautery. The specimen was sent for frozen section, confirming clear margins. A drain was placed, and the wound was closed in layers. The patient was extubated and transferred to recovery in stable condition.

Following the induction of anesthesia, a direct laryngoscopy was performed. A tumor was identified in the supraglottic area, specifically on the epiglottis, extending into the vallecula. The mass measured approximately 3 cm and appeared to infiltrate the pre-epiglottic space. CO2 laser was utilized to excise the tumor, ensuring a 5 mm margin of healthy tissue. During the procedure, care was taken to preserve the aryepiglottic folds. The specimen was sent for histopathological examination, confirming squamous cell carcinoma with clear margins. Hemostasis was achieved, and the patient was extubated without complications.

Under general anesthesia, the patient was prepped and draped for a transoral robotic-assisted procedure. Examination revealed a large, ulcerative lesion on the base of the tongue, extending bilaterally but predominantly on the right side. The tumor infiltrated the lingual tonsils and approached the vallecula. A robotic-assisted excision was performed, with wide local excision of the base of the tongue and bilateral lingual tonsillectomy. The procedure ensured clear lateral and deep margins. A nasogastric tube was placed for postoperative feeding. The patient was monitored in the recovery unit and showed stable vital signs.

Anesthesia was induced, and a complete examination of the larynx was performed using suspension laryngoscopy. A small, exophytic lesion was identified on the anterior two-thirds of the right vocal fold. The lesion measured about 1 cm in diameter and appeared well-circumscribed. A laser microdissection was used to excise the lesion, preserving the vocal fold's structural integrity. Postoperative frozen sections confirmed negative margins. The patient was extubated and transferred to recovery, with postoperative instructions to minimize vocal strain.

After induction of anesthesia, the patient was positioned supine, and a neck incision was made for an open approach to the hypopharynx. A large mass, originating from the left pyriform sinus and extending into the postcricoid area, was identified. The mass appeared to infiltrate the left arytenoid cartilage. A total laryngopharyngectomy was performed, with careful dissection to preserve surrounding vital structures. Reconstruction was carried out using a radial forearm free flap to restore the pharyngeal contour. Hemostasis was meticulously achieved, and a tracheostomy was performed. The patient was transferred to the ICU for postoperative monitoring.

Following induction of anesthesia and secure intubation, the patient was positioned supine, and the surgical field was prepped and draped. Initial inspection using a laryngoscope revealed a large exophytic mass occupying the anterior two-thirds of the right vocal fold, extending into the anterior commissure. The arytenoids appeared clear. A partial laryngectomy was performed using a CO2 laser set to 2 watts in continuous mode, carefully excising the mass with a 1 cm margin. Frozen sections were sent intraoperatively to ensure negative margins. Reconstruction was achieved using a radial forearm free flap to restore phonatory function. The patient tolerated the procedure well, and the airway was stable postoperatively.

Under general anesthesia, the patient was placed in a supine position. A wide exposure was obtained by using a Dingman mouth gag. Examination revealed a deeply invasive lesion at the left base of the tongue, extending posteriorly to involve the lingual tonsil tissue and anterior faucial pillar. A segmental mandibulectomy was performed to improve access. The lesion was resected en bloc with a 2 cm margin. Margins were checked with frozen section analysis and confirmed to be negative. Reconstruction of the defect was done using an anterolateral thigh flap. The patient was extubated in the operating room and transferred to recovery in stable condition.

Induction of anesthesia and intubation were successfully completed by the anesthesia team. The patient was positioned for optimal access to the oropharynx. Direct laryngoscopy revealed a circumferential mass involving the oropharyngeal wall, extending into the vallecula and base of the tongue on the left side. The lesion was infiltrating the lateral pharyngeal wall and extending posteriorly. A transoral robotic surgery (TORS) approach was utilized to excise the tumor with adequate margins. Intraoperative frozen sections confirmed clear margins. The patient was monitored closely for airway stability and extubated without complications.

The patient underwent general anesthesia, with successful intubation. The surgical field was prepped and exposed using a Weerda laryngoscope. An irregular mass was identified on the right lateral pharyngeal wall, extending into the piriform sinus and partially occluding the esophageal inlet. A lateral pharyngotomy approach was employed for access. The tumor was resected with a wide margin, and frozen section analysis verified negative margins. The surgical site was reconstructed using a pectoralis major myocutaneous flap. The patient was transferred to the ICU postoperatively for observation.

After induction of anesthesia, the patient was placed in a supine position. An incision was made along the anterior border of the sternocleidomastoid muscle to expose the cervical lymph node basin. Concurrently, a transoral examination revealed a carcinoma on the right glossoepiglottic fold, extending to the right lateral tongue base and vallecula. A radical neck dissection was performed to address regional lymphatic spread. The primary tumor was excised with a CO2 laser, ensuring a 1.5 cm margin. Intraoperative frozen sections confirmed all resection margins were clear. The patient was monitored in the post-anesthesia care unit and recovered without incident.

After induction of anesthesia and intubation by the anesthesiology team, the patient is positioned supine with slight neck extension. A thorough inspection of the oropharynx reveals a large ulcerative lesion occupying the left soft palate, extending to the uvula and involving the upper posterior pharyngeal wall. The lesion is friable and bleeds on contact. A decision is made to perform a wide local excision. The soft palate is resected with a 1.5 cm margin, and the specimen is sent for frozen section analysis. The posterior pharyngeal wall is also partially resected to ensure clear margins. Hemostasis is achieved using bipolar cautery. A nasopharyngeal flap is then elevated and rotated to cover the defect. R0 resection is confirmed by histopathology. The patient is extubated and transferred to recovery in stable condition.

The patient is prepared for surgery following standard anesthesia induction and intubation procedures. A transoral approach is utilized to access the oral cavity. A firm, exophytic mass is identified on the right lateral border of the tongue, measuring approximately 3.2 cm in diameter. The lesion appears to infiltrate the adjacent floor of the mouth. Resection is initiated by incising the mucosa around the tumor with a CO2 laser, ensuring a margin of at least 1 cm. The tumor is excised en bloc, along with portions of the adjacent lingual mucosa and submucosal tissues. The lingual nerve is preserved. Hemostasis is achieved using bipolar cautery. The specimen is submitted for pathological examination, and preliminary results indicate clear margins. The surgical site is irrigated and closed in layers.

The surgical procedure begins following the induction of general anesthesia and orotracheal intubation. Examination of the hypopharynx with a rigid laryngoscope reveals an infiltrative tumor originating from the left piriform sinus, extending to involve the postcricoid area and partially encroaching upon the laryngeal vestibule. The tumor appears fixed and moderately vascularized. A trans-cervical approach is planned for optimal exposure. The left neck is incised, and the tumor is carefully dissected away from the surrounding tissues, including the thyroid cartilage and hypopharyngeal musculature. A partial pharyngectomy is performed, and frozen section analysis confirms tumor-free margins. Reconstruction involves a radial forearm free flap. The patient tolerates the procedure well and is transferred to the ICU for postoperative care.

The patient is placed under general anesthesia with endotracheal intubation. An intraoperative examination with a direct laryngoscope reveals a large, exophytic lesion occupying the anterior two-thirds of the right vocal fold, extending to the anterior commissure. The lesion is highly vascular and impairs vocal fold mobility. Laser microsurgery is planned, and the tumor is ablated using a CO2 laser set at 2 watts in continuous mode. The lesion is meticulously vaporized, preserving as much of the normal tissue as possible. Hemostasis is achieved with topical epinephrine and microforceps. A biopsy is taken from the base of the lesion for histological evaluation, confirming squamous cell carcinoma with clear margins. The procedure concludes without complications, and the patient is extubated uneventfully.

Following anesthesia induction and secure intubation, an examination of the oral cavity with a mouth gag reveals a large ulcerating lesion on the floor of the mouth, extending laterally to the alveolar ridge and posteriorly to the base of the tongue. The lesion is firm to palpation and measures approximately 4 cm in its greatest dimension. A decision is made to perform a composite resection, including partial mandibulectomy. Using electrocautery, mucosal incisions are made around the lesion with a 1 cm margin. The mandible is resected using an oscillating saw. The specimen is sent for frozen section, and negative margins are confirmed. Reconstruction is achieved using a fibular free flap for mandibular continuity. The patient is transferred to recovery in stable condition, with plans for postoperative radiation therapy.

The patient was prepped and draped in the usual sterile fashion following the induction of anesthesia. An initial examination with the laryngoscope revealed a prominent mass occupying most of the vallecula, with extension towards the lingual surface of the epiglottis. The tumor appeared to infiltrate the lateral pharyngeal wall on the right side. A transoral laser microsurgery was planned, and the CO2 laser was employed to excise the mass with a safety margin. The excised tissue was sent for frozen section analysis, confirming malignancy with clear margins. Hemostasis was achieved using suction cautery. The patient tolerated the procedure well and was extubated in the operating room.

After induction of anesthesia, a thorough inspection of the oral cavity was performed. A large ulcerative lesion was observed on the dorsal surface of the tongue, extending to involve the lateral border on the right side. A partial glossectomy was performed with the use of a harmonic scalpel to minimize blood loss. Margins were marked and dissected carefully to ensure a clear boundary from the tumor. The defect was closed primarily with interrupted absorbable sutures. The specimen was sent for pathological evaluation, and the patient was monitored closely post-operatively with no immediate complications.

Under general anesthesia, the laryngoscope was inserted to evaluate the extent of the tumor. A massive growth was found occupying the glottic region, specifically affecting the anterior two-thirds of the right vocal fold and extending into the anterior commissure. The tumor appeared to infiltrate the subglottic area as well. A vertical partial laryngectomy was performed, excising the affected tissue while preserving the contralateral vocal fold. The resected tissue was sent for frozen section, confirming malignancy. A tracheostomy was performed for airway management post-surgery. The procedure concluded with successful hemostasis, and the patient was transferred to recovery.

The patient was positioned supine, and anesthesia was induced. A direct laryngoscopy revealed a large tumor mass located at the apex of the left piriform sinus, extending into the postcricoid region and involving the aryepiglottic fold. The tumor was noted to partially obstruct the laryngeal inlet. A trans-cervical approach was utilized to gain access to the tumor, and a wide excision was performed to ensure negative margins. Reconstruction of the laryngeal framework was achieved using a pectoralis major myocutaneous flap. Pathological examination confirmed the diagnosis, and the patient was extubated post-surgery with no immediate complications.

Following standard induction of anesthesia, a comprehensive inspection of the larynx showed a large exophytic lesion occupying the supraglottic region, primarily involving the epiglottis and extending into the arytenoids. The lesion also infiltrated the left false vocal cord. A partial supraglottic laryngectomy was performed, preserving the true vocal cords. The specimen was carefully dissected and sent for intraoperative histopathological consultation, which confirmed malignancy with all margins clear. The patient was managed post-operatively with a temporary tracheostomy to ensure airway patency. Recovery was uneventful, and the patient was monitored in the intensive care unit.

The patient was brought into the operating room and anesthesia was induced. A wide field of view was achieved using a retractor, and the oral cavity was inspected. A large ulcerative lesion was identified on the lateral border of the left tongue, extending to the floor of the mouth. Resection was initiated with a scalpel, ensuring a margin of 1 cm. The excision extended posteriorly to include part of the base of the tongue and laterally to the nearby sublingual gland. The specimen was sent for frozen section analysis, which confirmed the presence of squamous cell carcinoma with clear margins. Closure was achieved with absorbable sutures, and hemostasis was secured.

After induction of anesthesia and securing the airway, a direct laryngoscopy was performed. A bulky, infiltrative tumor was observed occupying the entire supraglottic larynx, extending into the glottis with partial fixation of the left vocal cord. The right vocal cord appeared edematous but mobile. Using a CO2 laser, the tumor was carefully debulked, starting from the anterior commissure and moving laterally. The resection was carried out with care to preserve as much of the vocal cord function as possible. Post-procedure examination ensured no residual tumor was visible.

Anesthesia was induced, and the patient was intubated. A neck dissection was performed on the left side. Attention was then turned to the oropharynx, where an exophytic mass was noted on the left side of the soft palate, extending to the tonsillar pillar. Using electrocautery, the mass was excised with clear margins. The resection extended to the posterior pharyngeal wall, and care was taken to avoid injury to the palatal muscles. The specimen was sent for pathological assessment, confirming moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma.

The patient was intubated after induction of general anesthesia. A laryngoscopic examination revealed a lesion involving the posterior wall of the hypopharynx, with invasion into the postcricoid area and partial encroachment onto the esophageal inlet. The decision was made to proceed with a partial pharyngectomy. An incision was made along the anterior neck, and the tumor was accessed. Resection involved removing the affected pharyngeal wall while preserving the laryngeal framework. Reconstruction was planned with a regional flap to ensure functional restoration.

With the patient under general anesthesia, a comprehensive endoscopic evaluation of the larynx was performed. A small, ulcerated lesion was identified on the right vocal fold, with superficial spread to the anterior commissure. A precise laser resection was conducted, ensuring complete removal of the lesion with minimal tissue damage. The lesion was excised with a 0.5 cm margin and sent for histopathological examination. Post-operative inspection confirmed clear margins and preservation of vocal fold mobility.

After successful induction of anesthesia and intubation by the anesthesia team, the patient was positioned supine with slight neck extension. Using a laryngoscope, an exophytic mass was identified at the left posterior wall of the oropharynx, extending to the lateral pharyngeal wall. The lesion encroached upon the lateral margin of the soft palate. Careful resection was performed using a monopolar cautery device, ensuring clear margins were obtained. The excised tissue was sent for frozen section analysis, which confirmed clear margins with no residual tumor. Hemostasis was achieved with bipolar cautery, and the area was irrigated and inspected for bleeding. The operation concluded with the placement of a nasopharyngeal airway to aid in postoperative recovery.

Following standard induction of anesthesia and endotracheal intubation, the patient was prepped and draped in a sterile fashion. A thorough examination with a laryngoscope revealed a fungating mass on the right vocal fold, extending into the subglottic region. The lesion partially obstructed the airway. A CO2 laser was utilized to excise the mass, starting at the anterior commissure and extending to the right vocal fold’s posterior margin. The resection was carried out meticulously to preserve the contralateral vocal fold. The subglottic extension was removed with a safety margin. Hemostasis was confirmed and the airway was cleared of debris before extubation.

Anesthesia was induced and the patient was intubated without complication. After sterile preparation, examination with a 0-degree scope revealed a lesion located at the base of the tongue on the left side, extending to the floor of the mouth. A wide local excision was performed, including partial resection of the left lingual nerve and associated sublingual salivary tissue. The lesion extended anteriorly towards the midline, necessitating the preservation of the contralateral floor of mouth structures. The surgical site was irrigated and inspected for hemostasis. A drain was placed in the left submandibular region to prevent fluid accumulation postoperatively.

The patient was brought to the operating room, anesthetized, and intubated. Examination revealed a large tumor occupying the entire left piriform sinus and extending into the postcricoid region and the proximal esophageal inlet. The tumor also involved the aryepiglottic fold. An incision was made to create a wide exposure of the larynx and hypopharynx. The tumor was debulked using sharp dissection and electrocautery, with care taken to preserve as much native laryngeal structure as possible. Frozen sections confirmed negative margins. Reconstruction involved the use of a radial forearm free flap to close the defect.

Under general anesthesia, the patient was intubated and prepped for surgery. The examination revealed a broad-based lesion at the anterior commissure, which extended to involve the right vocal fold and the subglottic region. Further inspection showed infiltration into the right aryepiglottic fold. Resection commenced at the anterior commissure using a CO2 laser, with subsequent dissection of the right vocal fold. The subglottic portion was excised with care to maintain airway patency. The aryepiglottic fold was resected with a safety margin. Hemostasis was achieved, and the airway was patent upon completion of the procedure.

Induction of anesthesia performed by the anesthesiology team, followed by endoscopic examination using a laryngoscope. On inspection, a noticeable mass is observed on the right side of the oropharynx, extending into the tonsillar fossa and approaching the base of the tongue. The mass is exophytic, with ulcerated areas and induration palpated upon examination. A biopsy is taken for histopathological analysis. The surgical team proceeds with a transoral robotic resection of the tumor, achieving hemostasis using electrocautery. Margins are assessed and appear clear on frozen section analysis. The procedure concludes with the placement of a nasogastric tube for postoperative nutrition.

Anesthesia induction and endotracheal intubation are successfully completed. The patient is positioned, and a detailed examination of the larynx is conducted using a fiberoptic laryngoscope. A large, infiltrative mass occupying the right piriform sinus is identified, with extension into the postcricoid region and partial obstruction of the airway. The upper esophagus is also involved, with the mass spreading towards the cervical esophagus. Surgical resection is initiated with a trans-cervical approach, and the mass is carefully excised with clear margins confirmed by intraoperative frozen section. Reconstruction of the pharyngeal defect is performed using a radial forearm free flap.

Following induction of general anesthesia, the surgical area is prepped and draped. The patient undergoes direct laryngoscopy, revealing a malignant tumor on the left vocal cord, extending to the anterior commissure and partially involving the arytenoid cartilage. The lesion exhibits a mixed endophytic and exophytic growth pattern. A CO2 laser is utilized to perform a cordectomy, with meticulous attention to maintaining hemostasis and preserving as much function as possible. The specimen is sent for pathological examination, and the surgical field is closed with absorbable sutures.

The patient undergoes anesthesia induction, and the surgical team performs a transoral examination. A bulky lesion is identified on the lateral wall of the oropharynx, extending into the vallecula and partially involving the epiglottis. The mass is noted to be friable with areas of necrosis. A transoral laser microsurgery approach is selected, and the tumor is carefully resected with the assistance of a microscope. Frozen sections confirm negative margins. The surgical site is irrigated, and hemostasis is achieved. A temporary tracheostomy is performed to secure the airway postoperatively.

After successful induction of anesthesia and endotracheal intubation, a thorough oropharyngeal examination is conducted. A lesion is identified in the left tonsillar region, with infiltration into the soft palate and extending towards the lateral pharyngeal wall. The tumor is biopsied, and resection is commenced using a combined transoral and trans-cervical approach. The soft palate is partially resected, and the defect is repaired with a local mucosal flap. Intraoperative frozen sections confirm negative resection margins. Postoperative care includes placement of a nasopharyngeal airway and monitoring in the intensive care unit.

After successful induction of anesthesia and intubation, the oral cavity was accessed with a mouth gag. Examination revealed a large ulcerative lesion on the left lateral border of the tongue extending anteriorly towards the tip and posteriorly towards the base. The lesion was firm and fixed, with evidence of infiltration into the adjacent floor of the mouth. A wide local excision was performed with a margin of 2 cm around the lesion. The resection included partial removal of the floor of the mouth and necessitated the excision of the ipsilateral submandibular duct. Hemostasis was achieved, and the surgical site was closed with interrupted sutures. Specimen sent for histopathological examination confirmed squamous cell carcinoma with clear margins.

Following induction of anesthesia and securing the airway with a cuffed endotracheal tube, a direct laryngoscopy was performed. The inspection revealed an exophytic, ulcerated mass occupying the entire right piriform sinus with extension into the postcricoid area and partial involvement of the arytenoid cartilage. The mass also encroached upon the right vocal fold, causing limited mobility. A CO2 laser was utilized for precise resection of the mass. The tissue was excised in blocks, and the resection margins were sent for frozen section analysis, which indicated clear margins. The patient was extubated successfully post-procedure and transferred to recovery.

Anesthesia was induced, and intubation was performed without complications. A transoral approach was used to enter the oropharynx. Examination under magnification revealed a polypoidal mass originating from the left tonsillar fossa, extending to the soft palate and the posterior pharyngeal wall. The mass was excised using electrocautery with care taken to preserve surrounding structures. The resection was completed with a safety margin, and the specimen was sent for histopathological evaluation. Hemostasis was achieved, and the patient was extubated and observed in the recovery room with no immediate complications.

After induction of anesthesia and securing the airway, a laryngoscopy was performed. A large tumor was identified in the supraglottic larynx, extending into the glottis, with significant involvement of the left vocal fold and partial invasion of the anterior commissure. The tumor also extended superiorly into the aryepiglottic fold. A partial laryngectomy was performed, removing the tumor en bloc with preservation of the uninvolved right vocal fold. Laser excision was utilized to ensure precision. The surgical margins were confirmed to be clear on frozen section analysis. Postoperative hemostasis was satisfactory, and the patient was monitored in the ICU.

The patient was anesthetized and intubated, and a thorough examination of the oral cavity was conducted. A large, fungating mass was observed on the right base of the tongue, extending into the vallecula and partially invading the lingual surface of the epiglottis. A transoral robotic-assisted surgery was performed for precise excision of the tumor with negative margins. The resection included the base of the tongue and part of the epiglottic tissue. Frozen sections were taken intraoperatively to confirm margin clearance. Post-resection, the hemostasis was achieved with bipolar cautery, and the patient was extubated uneventfully.

The patient was placed in a supine position and anesthesia was induced successfully. The oral cavity was exposed using a mouth retractor, and the tumor was visualized at the dorsal aspect of the left lateral tongue. The lesion extended posteriorly to involve the base of the tongue and the anterior tonsillar pillar. A wide local excision was performed with a margin of 1.5 cm. The resection included partial glossectomy and removal of the affected tonsillar tissue. The surgical specimen was sent for frozen section analysis, which confirmed negative margins. Hemostasis was achieved, and the wound was closed in layers. The patient tolerated the procedure well with no immediate complications.

Anesthesia was induced, and the patient was intubated without difficulty. A direct laryngoscopy was performed, revealing a large exophytic tumor occupying the entire supraglottic region, extending from the epiglottis to the aryepiglottic folds bilaterally. The lesion was noted to infiltrate the left vocal fold and partially obstruct the airway. CO2 laser resection was carried out, starting at the anterior commissure and progressing laterally to achieve complete tumor removal. The surgical field was adequately suctioned, and the patient was extubated and transferred to recovery in stable condition.

Upon induction of anesthesia, a full examination of the oropharynx was conducted using a flexible endoscope. The patient presented with a bulky mass located in the right vallecula, extending to the lateral pharyngeal wall and involving the hypopharynx. The tumor was excised using electrocautery, with care taken to avoid injury to the surrounding structures. The lesion was dissected away from the prevertebral fascia and resected en bloc. The surgical site was irrigated, and meticulous hemostasis was achieved. Specimen margins were confirmed to be negative on frozen section analysis.

Anesthesia was induced and the patient was positioned for optimal access to the larynx. Using rigid laryngoscopy, a cauliflower-like mass was identified on the right true vocal cord, extending to the anterior commissure and partially involving the false vocal cord. The tumor was excised using microdebrider technology, ensuring preservation of the surrounding laryngeal structures. Hemostasis was achieved with bipolar cautery. The patient’s vocal function was preserved, and they were extubated uneventfully, recovering well from the surgical procedure.

The patient was anesthetized and a comprehensive examination of the hypopharynx was conducted using a laryngoscope. A circumferential tumor was noted in the left piriform sinus, extending medially to the postcricoid area and superiorly to the arytenoid region. Transoral laser microsurgery was employed to resect the lesion with a 1 cm margin. The procedure included partial resection of the posterior pharyngeal wall to ensure complete tumor clearance. Intraoperative frozen sections confirmed negative margins. The patient's airway was managed carefully postoperatively, and they were monitored in the intensive care unit.

Following induction of anesthesia and intubation, we proceeded with a careful inspection using a flexible laryngoscope. The patient presented with a significant exophytic tumor at the left base of the tongue, extending to the midline and involving the left vallecula. The tumor also encroached upon the lateral pharyngeal wall. A laser was employed to perform a precise resection with a 1.5 cm margin around the tumor, ensuring complete removal while preserving critical structures. The resected specimen was sent for frozen section analysis, which confirmed negative margins. Hemostasis was achieved using bipolar cautery. Postoperative assessment showed satisfactory airway patency and no complications.

Under general anesthesia, the patient was positioned, and the mouth was opened using a mouth gag. A detailed examination revealed a lesion occupying the anterior two-thirds of the right vocal fold, with extension into the anterior commissure. The lesion demonstrated invasive characteristics into the subglottic area. A CO2 laser was utilized for precise excision of the tumor, ensuring preservation of the contralateral vocal fold. Frozen sections were obtained intraoperatively, confirming clear resection margins. Post-resection, the vocal fold mobility was assessed and deemed satisfactory, with no immediate postoperative bleeding observed.

The patient was anesthetized and intubated. A transoral approach was used to access the oropharynx, where a large ulcerative lesion was identified on the soft palate extending towards the tonsillar pillar on the left side. The tumor infiltrated the posterior pillar and partially involved the base of the tongue. A wide local excision was performed, and the specimen was sent for frozen section analysis. Margins were confirmed to be free of tumor. A reconstruction of the defect was completed using a local flap technique. The patient tolerated the procedure well with stable vital signs throughout.

After induction of anesthesia and securing the airway, a rigid endoscope was introduced to examine the hypopharynx. A large mass was identified occupying the left piriform sinus, extending into the postcricoid area and partially involving the aryepiglottic fold. The decision was made to proceed with a partial pharyngectomy, preserving as much of the laryngeal framework as possible. Tissue samples were taken for intraoperative pathology consultation, which verified negative margins. A pectoralis major flap was utilized for reconstruction, providing robust coverage and support to the pharyngoesophageal junction.

The patient underwent general anesthesia and was positioned supine. A thorough examination with a laryngoscope revealed a circumferential mass in the subglottic region, extending into the upper trachea. The lesion caused significant airway narrowing. A tracheostomy was performed to secure the airway prior to initiating tumor resection. The tumor was excised using a combination of sharp dissection and electrocautery, ensuring clear margins. The resected tissue was sent for pathological examination, confirming complete resection. A primary anastomosis of the trachea was performed to restore anatomical continuity.

The patient was brought to the operating room, and anesthesia was induced without complications. A mouth gag was applied, and the oral cavity was exposed. A bulky tumor was noted occupying the left lateral tongue, measuring approximately 3 cm in diameter. The tumor extended posteriorly towards the base of the tongue. Careful dissection was performed to achieve a 1 cm margin of healthy tissue around the tumor. The lesion was excised en bloc, including a portion of the adjacent floor of the mouth. Hemostasis was achieved using bipolar cautery. The specimen was sent for frozen section, which confirmed clear margins. The wound was closed primarily with interrupted sutures. The patient tolerated the procedure well and was extubated in the operating room.

Following induction of general anesthesia and endotracheal intubation, a direct laryngoscopy was performed using the Kleinsasser laryngoscope. An irregularly shaped tumor was identified occupying the entire supraglottic larynx, extending into the left aryepiglottic fold and partially obstructing the airway. The tumor was carefully excised using a carbon dioxide laser set at 2 watts, continuous mode. The resection extended to the false vocal cords bilaterally. The patient was extubated and monitored in the recovery room with no immediate complications.

After intubation and induction of anesthesia, a comprehensive panendoscopy was performed. Anesthesia was induced smoothly, and the patient was intubated. Following the induction of anesthesia and intubation, the surgical field was prepared with sterile washing and draping. Anesthesia was induced, and the patient was intubated. The surgical area was prepped with antiseptic solution and draped in a sterile fashion. The patient is prepped and draped in the usual sterile fashion following induction of anesthesia. A direct laryngoscopy is performed using a Kleinsasser tube. The patient is anesthetized and intubated. Following induction of anesthesia and securement of the airway via intubation, the surgical team commenced with a thorough inspection utilizing a 0° endoscope. Under general anesthesia, after intubation, the patient was positioned for optimal access to the oropharynx. The patient was prepared and positioned supine on the operating table. Anesthesia was induced, and intubation was achieved without complications. Upon inspection with the endoscope, a significant exophytic lesion was observed on the right lateral aspect of the oropharynx extending to the soft palate and involving the posterior pharyngeal wall. The lesion was friable and bled easily upon contact. A resection was performed using electrocautery, ensuring clear margins. The specimen was sent for pathology. Hemostasis was achieved with bipolar cautery. The area was irrigated and inspected for any residual tumor. No further lesions were noted. The patient was extubated and transferred to recovery in stable condition.

After successful induction of anesthesia, the patient was intubated and positioned for optimal access to the oral cavity. A thorough examination was performed using a direct laryngoscope. A large ulcerative mass was identified on the left lateral border of the tongue, extending towards the base and infiltrating the adjacent floor of the mouth. Using a scalpel, a wide local excision was carried out, including a portion of the tongue and the involved floor of the mouth, while maintaining a 2 cm margin. The lingual nerve was preserved. Specimens were labeled and sent for histopathological evaluation. Hemostasis was confirmed, and the surgical site was closed using absorbable sutures. The patient was extubated and sent to the recovery room in good condition.

Induction of anesthesia was completed smoothly, followed by intubation using a reinforced endotracheal tube. The neck was extended and prepped for a comprehensive examination. A direct laryngoscopy revealed a suspicious lesion on the right vocal cord, which appeared erythematous and irregular with partial involvement of the anterior commissure. Utilizing a CO2 laser, the lesion was carefully excised with precision, ensuring the preservation of normal surrounding tissue. The specimen was retrieved and sent for frozen section analysis, confirming malignancy consistent with squamous cell carcinoma. Meticulous hemostasis was achieved, and the procedure concluded without complications. The patient was extubated and awakened in the operating theater before being transferred to recovery.

The patient was anesthetized and intubated nasally for enhanced access to the oral cavity. A tongue depressor and cheek retractor were used to expose the surgical field. A large fungating mass was noted at the base of the tongue, extending posteriorly to the vallecula and encroaching upon the epiglottis. A transoral robotic-assisted resection was performed, allowing for precise excision of the tumor with adequate margins. The specimen was sent for frozen section, which confirmed complete excision of malignant tissue. Minor bleeding was controlled with electrocautery. The surgical site was irrigated thoroughly, and no further abnormalities were identified. The patient was extubated and observed in the post-anesthesia care unit with stable vital signs.

Following anesthesia induction and orotracheal intubation, the patient was positioned supine. A laryngoscope was used to visualize the larynx, where a large exophytic tumor was identified originating from the left piriform sinus and extending into the aryepiglottic fold and involving the postcricoid region. The tumor was resected using a combination of cold instruments and suction cautery. The resection was challenging due to the tumor's proximity to the arytenoid cartilage and vocal ligament. Tissue specimens were sent for pathological assessment. Hemostasis was achieved, and the surgical field was inspected for any residual tumor. The patient tolerated the procedure well and was returned to the recovery area for postoperative monitoring.

Following induction of anesthesia, the patient's mouth was opened using a mouth retractor, and the tongue was secured. A detailed inspection of the oropharynx was conducted, revealing a tumor involving the right lateral wall of the oropharynx with extension into the tonsillar region and soft palate. The tumor was exophytic with some ulceration noted. A wide local excision was performed with a safety margin of 1 cm, including partial resection of the right tonsil and adjacent soft palate. Frozen section analysis confirmed clear margins. Reconstruction of the soft palate was completed using local mucosal flaps. Postoperative hemostasis was secured, and the area was irrigated before closure.

Upon induction of anesthesia, the patient was intubated, and the larynx was visualized using a laryngoscope. A large, irregular mass was observed occupying the left supraglottic region, extending inferiorly to the true vocal cords and involving the anterior commissure. The mass exhibited both exophytic and infiltrative growth patterns. A laser cordectomy was performed, removing the affected vocal cords and part of the supraglottic larynx. The surgical area was closely monitored for bleeding, and appropriate hemostatic measures were applied. The specimen was sent for histopathological examination. The patient was extubated after achieving stable hemostasis.

After successful induction of anesthesia, a mouth retractor was used to access the oral cavity. Examination revealed a firm, ulcerative lesion on the left lateral border of the tongue, with infiltration toward the base of the tongue and involvement of adjacent floor of mouth tissues. A partial glossectomy was performed to excise the lesion with a 1.5 cm margin. The dissection extended into the floor of the mouth, and a portion of the submandibular duct was resected. The surgical site was reconstructed using a radial forearm free flap for optimal functional recovery. Margins were clear on frozen section analysis.

The patient was positioned supine, and anesthesia was induced. Using a Kleinsasser laryngoscope, a tumor was identified in the right piriform sinus, extending superiorly to involve the aryepiglottic fold and inferiorly into the upper esophageal sphincter. A lateral pharyngotomy was performed to gain access. The tumor was resected in total, including part of the postcricoid region. Reconstruction was accomplished with a pectoralis major myocutaneous flap to maintain structural integrity and function. Hemostasis was meticulously achieved, and the patient was extubated in stable condition.

Under general anesthesia, direct laryngoscopy revealed a large mass in the right glottic larynx, extending into the subglottic region. The tumor was obstructing more than half of the airway, necessitating a tracheostomy for airway management. A vertical partial laryngectomy was performed, including resection of the right vocal cord, part of the thyroid cartilage, and the adjacent subglottic tissues. The defect was reconstructed using local tissue to preserve laryngeal function. Hemostasis was secured, and the patient was transferred to the recovery unit with the tracheostomy in situ for postoperative airway management.

Induction of anesthesia and endotracheal intubation performed by the anesthesiology team. The patient is positioned supine, and the head is reclined. A mouth retractor is inserted to provide optimal visibility. Upon inspection with a 0° endoscope, a large ulcerative lesion is observed on the left lateral aspect of the oropharynx extending into the soft palate. The lesion infiltrates the adjacent tonsillar tissue and progresses posteriorly toward the base of the tongue. The tumor is excised with a 1 cm safety margin using a monopolar cautery. The resection extends into the left tonsillar fossa and the posterior pillar. Specimens are sent for frozen section analysis, confirming clear margins. Hemostasis is achieved, and the surgical site is irrigated with saline before closure.

General anesthesia is induced, and the patient is intubated. Inspection with a Kleinsasser direct laryngoscope reveals a large, exophytic mass located on the anterior two-thirds of the right vocal fold, extending into the anterior commissure. The lesion also involves the right false vocal fold. The remainder of the larynx, including the subglottic region, appears normal. CO2 laser resection is initiated, focusing on the right vocal fold. The tumor is excised en bloc, and the specimen is sent for histopathological examination. Hemostasis is achieved using bipolar cautery. Postoperative assessment confirms adequate vocal fold mobility.

Under general anesthesia, the patient undergoes a surgical exploration of the oral cavity. A large, indurated, and ulcerated tumor is identified on the right lateral border of the tongue, involving the middle third. The lesion extends to the floor of the mouth, crossing the midline. A wide local excision is performed, including partial resection of the right lingual nerve and submandibular duct. The resection also involves the anterior floor of the mouth. Specimens are sent for frozen section analysis, which confirms tumor-free margins. Reconstruction is performed with a radial forearm free flap. The surgical field is irrigated, and hemostasis is confirmed.

Following induction of general anesthesia and endotracheal intubation, a thorough examination of the hypopharynx is carried out using a rigid esophagoscope. A tumor is identified in the postcricoid region, extending into the left piriform sinus and infiltrating the aryepiglottic fold. The mass also affects the posterior cricoid area. A transoral laser microsurgery approach is utilized to excise the tumor. The resection is carried out in a piecemeal fashion, ensuring clear margins. Specimens are submitted for histopathological analysis, confirming the presence of squamous cell carcinoma. The surgical site is inspected for bleeding, which is controlled with cautery.

The patient is placed under general anesthesia and intubated. A flexible fiberoptic laryngoscope is used to assess the airway. A large, exophytic tumor is noted on the left arytenoid cartilage, extending into the interarytenoid space and involving the left vocal fold. The lesion infiltrates the posterior glottis but spares the right vocal fold entirely. A laser-assisted endoscopic resection is performed, ensuring preservation of the contralateral vocal fold. The specimen is sent for immediate frozen section, confirming negative margins. Hemostasis is achieved, and the airway is reassessed to ensure patency before the patient is extubated.

Induction of anesthesia and patient positioning were performed by the anesthesiology team. A comprehensive inspection of the oral cavity and oropharynx revealed a fungating mass occupying the right tonsillar fossa, extending medially to involve the soft palate and laterally to the right lateral pharyngeal wall. The lesion was biopsied, confirming a diagnosis of squamous cell carcinoma. A transoral robotic-assisted resection was planned. The robotic arms were inserted, and careful dissection was performed to completely excise the tumor with a surrounding safety margin. The resection extended laterally to the pharyngeal wall and medially to the uvula. Hemostasis was achieved, and the specimen was sent for pathological evaluation. All margins were clear on frozen section analysis, confirming an R0 resection.

The patient was brought to the operating room and anesthesia was induced. A rigid laryngoscope was used to visualize the larynx, revealing a significant exophytic growth on the right vocal cord extending into the subglottic region. After securing the airway with a reinforced endotracheal tube, a microlaryngoscopic approach was employed. The tumor was sent for frozen section, which confirmed clear margins. Post-procedure inspection showed intact mucosa and satisfactory hemostasis.

Under general anesthesia, the patient was prepared for a comprehensive examination and potential resection of a lesion located at the base of the tongue. Anesthesia was induced, and the patient was positioned supine. A direct laryngoscopy revealed a large tumorous lesion on the posterior third of the tongue, extending into the oropharynx. The patient was anesthetized and positioned supine with the neck extended. After induction of anesthesia and securing of the airway, the patient's neck is extended, and a transoral approach is utilized.
