Initial inspection using direct laryngoscopy revealed a large ulcerated mass on the right lateral wall of the oropharynx, extending to involve the soft palate and the anterior tonsillar pillar. The tumor was excised with a 1 cm margin using electrocautery, which included part of the soft palate and the right tonsil. The defect was reconstructed using a radial forearm free flap. Frozen sections confirmed clear margins. Postoperative bleeding was controlled with meticulous hemostasis, and the patient was extubated without complications.

The patient was prepared for surgery under general anesthesia. Initial examination with a laryngoscope showed a significant lesion occupying the epiglottis and extending into the supraglottic larynx. Partial laryngectomy was performed to excise the tumor, preserving the arytenoids and vocal cords. The lesion was carefully dissected with a CO2 laser, maintaining a clear margin. The surrounding tissue was re-evaluated for any signs of residual disease, and frozen sections confirmed an R0 resection. The surgical site was closed with absorbable sutures, and the patient was transferred to the recovery unit in stable condition.

Following induction of anesthesia, a thorough inspection using a laryngoscope revealed an infiltrative mass on the right lateral border of the tongue, extending to the floor of the mouth and involving the right submandibular duct. A wide local excision was performed, removing the affected portion of the tongue and the involved floor of the mouth tissue. The submandibular gland was also excised due to tumor involvement. Margins were sent for frozen section and confirmed clear. Reconstruction of the defect was accomplished using a local rotational flap. Hemostasis was achieved, and a drain was placed before wound closure.

After the induction of anesthesia, the patient was positioned, and the neck was prepared and draped. Examination with a laryngoscope identified a large exophytic tumor on the left piriform sinus, which extended to the aryepiglottic fold and into the postcricoid space. A combined pharyngolaryngectomy was necessary to achieve complete resection of the tumor. The surgical specimen was sent for pathological evaluation, and intraoperative frozen sections confirmed tumor-free margins. A tracheostomy was performed for postoperative airway management. The neck was closed in layers after placing a drain, and the patient was transferred to the ICU for close monitoring.

Under general anesthesia, a direct laryngoscopic examination revealed a proliferative lesion on the anterior two-thirds of the left vocal cord, extending to the anterior commissure. The lesion was excised using microlaryngeal instruments, following which a laser was employed to excise the remaining tumor from the anterior commissure. Care was taken to preserve the contralateral vocal cord and ensure a clear margin. The excised tissue was sent for histopathology, which confirmed squamous cell carcinoma with clear surgical margins. The patient was extubated post-procedure and monitored in the recovery room.

Following induction of anesthesia, direct laryngoscopy was performed using the Kleinsasser tube. Examination revealed a mass located at the anterior portion of the epiglottis, extending to the aryepiglottic fold on the left side. The lesion appears exophytic and irregular, with involvement of the left arytenoid cartilage. The right side of the larynx remains unaffected. Laser excision was conducted using a CO2 laser with a power setting of 2 watts in continuous mode. The excised tissue was sent for histopathological examination which confirmed squamous cell carcinoma. Resection margins were clear, achieving an R0 resection. The patient tolerated the procedure well and will be monitored for potential recurrence.

After induction of anesthesia, we performed a comprehensive endoscopic assessment. The examination revealed a significant ulcerative lesion on the right lateral border of the tongue, approximately 3 cm in diameter, extending to the floor of the mouth. The lesion was excised with a 1.5 cm margin, including partial resection of the right lateral floor of the mouth and lingual nerve. The submandibular duct was preserved. Frozen section analysis confirmed clear margins. A neck dissection was subsequently performed, with removal of levels I-III lymph nodes. All excised tissues were sent for histopathological evaluation, which confirmed squamous cell carcinoma and no lymph node metastasis.

Anesthesia was induced, and the patient was intubated without difficulty. Initial inspection using a flexible laryngoscope showed a deeply infiltrative mass at the base of the tongue, extending posteriorly to the vallecula and involving the lingual tonsil area. The mass was excised via transoral robotic surgery, ensuring a margin of 2 cm wherever feasible. The tumor extended into the lateral pharyngeal wall, and additional resection was required. The surgical field was examined for hemostasis, and the resected specimens were sent for pathological assessment. Margins were negative for tumor; however, the proximity to critical structures will necessitate close follow-up.

Following successful induction of anesthesia, direct visualization with a 70-degree endoscope revealed a large fungating mass at the left piriform sinus, extending to the postcricoid area and partially obstructing the esophageal entrance. The mass was resected using a combined transoral laser and open approach. The resection included part of the left arytenoid and involved the cricoid cartilage. Frozen sections confirmed clear margins, achieving an R0 resection. The patient was extubated postoperatively in stable condition and will require adjuvant radiotherapy due to the aggressive nature of the tumor.

Anesthesia was induced, and a comprehensive examination with a rigid laryngoscope was conducted. A mass was identified on the right vocal fold, measuring approximately 1.5 cm, extending to the anterior commissure but not crossing to the contralateral side. The lesion was excised using a CO2 laser, with care taken to preserve the vocalis muscle to maintain function. Histopathology confirmed squamous cell carcinoma with negative margins. The patient was extubated without complications and was advised on voice rest and follow-up with speech therapy to optimize vocal recovery.

The patient was brought into the operating room and anesthesia was induced smoothly, followed by intubation. A thorough inspection was performed using a laryngoscope, revealing a prominent mass on the right vocal fold, extending anteriorly to the anterior commissure and posteriorly towards the arytenoid. The mass was exophytic and partially ulcerated. A CO2 laser was used to carefully excise the tumor, ensuring clear margins. The anterior commissure was treated meticulously to preserve the structure and function of the larynx. The specimen was sent for histopathological examination. Hemostasis was achieved, and the patient was extubated successfully with no immediate complications noted.

After induction of general anesthesia, the patient was positioned supine with neck extension. A direct laryngoscopy was performed, identifying a mass on the base of the tongue extending to the right lateral border. The mass appeared nodular with irregular borders and was infiltrating the adjacent floor of the mouth. Utilizing a monopolar cautery, the tumor was resected with a 1.5 cm margin, and the excision extended to the lingual tonsil area. Frozen section analysis confirmed clear margins. The area was irrigated and inspected for bleeding, with absorbable sutures used to secure the mucosal edges. The patient tolerated the procedure well.

Anesthesia was initiated, and the patient was intubated without difficulty. Examination with an endoscope revealed a large mass occupying the left piriform sinus, extending superiorly to the aryepiglottic fold and infiltrating the left arytenoid. The tumor also involved the postcricoid region, indicating extensive disease. A transoral laser microsurgery approach was utilized to debulk the tumor and achieve maximum preservation of surrounding structures. Frozen sections confirmed tumor-free margins. The procedure concluded with hemostasis and the placement of a nasogastric tube for postoperative feeding. The patient was transferred to recovery in stable condition.

The patient was anesthetized and positioned with the neck slightly flexed. An intraoperative endoscopic evaluation revealed a tumor located at the left lateral oropharynx, involving the tonsillar fossa and extending into the soft palate. The tumor was resected using a harmonic scalpel, taking care to preserve the uvula and minimize damage to surrounding tissue. Due to the tumor's proximity to the palatine arch, additional care was taken to ensure hemostasis. A nasopharyngeal stent was placed to support the airway during recovery. The surgical site was closed primarily, and the patient was extubated and transferred to the recovery room.

With the patient under general anesthesia, a panendoscopy was performed to locate the tumor. A significant mass was found at the glottic and subglottic levels, primarily affecting the right vocal fold and extending into the subglottic space. The tumor was causing partial obstruction of the airway. Resection was accomplished using a CO2 laser, ensuring careful dissection around the cricoid cartilage to maintain airway integrity. The vocal fold was reconstructed using a mucosal flap technique to optimize postoperative voice quality. Post-resection, the airway was patent with no immediate complications. The patient was extubated successfully and monitored closely in the recovery unit.

Anesthesia was induced, and the patient was intubated by the anesthesiology team. The oral cavity was opened using a mouth retractor, and a detailed inspection revealed a large, ulcerative lesion on the lateral border of the tongue, extending posteriorly to involve the floor of the mouth and reaching the base of the tongue. The lesion was biopsied, and frozen section confirmed squamous cell carcinoma. A partial glossectomy was performed with resection margins extending to the midline and dorsally to include a portion of the base of the tongue. Resection of the left lingual nerve and submandibular duct was necessary due to tumor infiltration. The surgical field was thoroughly examined, and all resection margins were confirmed to be free of tumor cells, achieving an R0 resection. Hemostasis was ensured, and reconstruction was performed using a radial forearm free flap.

The patient was placed under general anesthesia, and the endoscope was introduced for a detailed examination of the oropharynx. A bulky mass was identified on the right lateral wall of the oropharynx, extending into the tonsillar fossa and involving the soft palate. Biopsy confirmed malignancy. The surgical approach involved a transoral robotic-assisted resection of the oropharyngeal tumor. The tumor was excised with adequate margins, and the resection extended superiorly to include part of the soft palate and inferiorly to the posterior pharyngeal wall. The resected specimen was sent for histopathological examination, and all margins were found to be negative. Reconstruction of the soft palate was carried out using local tissue advancement.

After induction of anesthesia, the patient was intubated, and direct laryngoscopy was performed. Visualization revealed a large exophytic lesion occupying the entire left pyriform sinus, with invasion into the postcricoid area and the hypopharyngeal wall. The tumor further extended to the left vocal fold, causing significant impairment of vocal fold mobility. A combined approach of laser resection and open partial pharyngectomy was utilized to remove the tumor. The surgical resection included the left pyriform sinus, part of the postcricoid region, and the involved vocal fold. Reconstruction was performed using a pectoralis major myocutaneous flap to cover the defect. Final pathology confirmed clear margins.

Induction of general anesthesia was performed, followed by careful endoscopic examination of the larynx. A sessile tumor was identified on the right true vocal fold, extending into the anterior commissure and partially infiltrating the subglottic space. The decision was made to proceed with a CO2 laser excision of the tumor. The lesion was meticulously vaporized, starting from the anterior commissure and moving laterally to ensure complete removal while preserving as much normal tissue as possible. Post-resection, the surgical site was examined, and hemostasis was achieved. The patient tolerated the procedure well, and postoperative recovery was uneventful.

The patient underwent induction of anesthesia and was positioned for transoral examination. Using a laryngoscope, a large, infiltrative mass was visualized occupying the vallecula and extending to the epiglottis and right aryepiglottic fold. The mass was biopsied, confirming squamous cell carcinoma. Surgical resection involved a partial pharyngectomy and resection of the epiglottis. The tumor extended into the pre-epiglottic space, requiring resection of the right aryepiglottic fold and part of the epiglottis. Careful dissection was performed to preserve the contralateral structures. The resected specimen was sent for histological analysis, confirming negative margins. A tracheostomy was performed to ensure airway patency during the healing process.

The patient was placed under general anesthesia, and intubation was performed smoothly. A direct laryngoscopy was conducted using a 0° endoscope. An exophytic mass was observed on the right side of the base of the tongue, extending into the vallecula and partially infiltrating the epiglottis. The mass measured approximately 3.5 cm in diameter. The surgical team proceeded with a transoral robotic-assisted resection, ensuring clear margins on all sides. The specimen was sent for frozen section analysis, confirming negative margins. Hemostasis was achieved, and the surgical site was closed with absorbable sutures. The patient tolerated the procedure well and was extubated in the recovery room.

Following induction of anesthesia, a thorough examination of the hypopharynx and larynx was conducted using a 30° endoscope. A large ulcerative lesion was identified in the posterior wall of the hypopharynx, extending into the left piriform sinus and partially involving the postcricoid area. The lesion was approximately 4 cm in length and was causing significant obstruction. Surgical excision was initiated with a lateral pharyngotomy approach. The tumor was carefully dissected and removed in its entirety, with clear margins confirmed by frozen section. The defect was reconstructed using a radial forearm free flap. The procedure was completed without complications.

Anesthesia was induced, and the patient was intubated without difficulty. A 45° telescope was used to inspect the oral cavity and oropharynx. A fungating mass was located on the lateral aspect of the left tonsillar pillar, extending to the soft palate and infiltrating the anterior faucial arch. The mass measured approximately 2 cm. The surgical team performed a transoral laser microsurgery to excise the mass, ensuring a wide margin to achieve R0 resection. Hemostasis was achieved with bipolar cautery. The patient was successfully extubated and transported to the recovery area in stable condition.

Under general anesthesia, the surgical team performed a comprehensive laryngoscopic examination. A sessile tumor was identified on the anterior two-thirds of the left vocal cord, measuring approximately 1.8 cm, with involvement of the anterior commissure. The tumor caused restricted mobility of the left vocal cord. A CO2 laser was used to excise the tumor completely, maintaining a conservative approach to preserve vocal function. Margins were confirmed to be clear on frozen section analysis. The patient was extubated post-procedure and monitored for airway patency.

The patient was anesthetized, and a rigid esophagoscope was used to examine the esophagus and hypopharynx. A bulky mass was discovered in the left piriform sinus, extending laterally into the lateral pharyngeal wall and medially to the aryepiglottic fold. The tumor measured 3.2 cm and was causing significant dysphagia. A lateral pharyngotomy was performed to access and excise the tumor. The resection margins were assessed with frozen section, which indicated clear margins. The pharyngeal defect was reconstructed using a local mucosal flap. The procedure was completed without incident, and the patient was transferred to the ICU for postoperative care.

Induction of general anesthesia followed by intubation was achieved without complications. The patient was positioned supine and the neck was extended to allow optimal access. Examination with a laryngoscope revealed a fungating mass occupying the entire right supraglottic larynx and extending into the right aryepiglottic fold. The right false vocal cord was also involved, although the true vocal cords appeared uninvolved. A CO2 laser was employed to excise the mass, ensuring clear margins. Frozen sections confirmed R0 resection. Careful inspection post-resection showed no residual tumor, and hemostasis was achieved with bipolar cautery. The patient tolerated the procedure well and was extubated in the operating room.

Anesthesia was induced and intubation was facilitated by anesthesiology. The patient was placed supine, and the surgical area was prepped and draped in the usual sterile fashion. Examination with a mirror revealed a broad-based ulcerative lesion on the left lateral border of the tongue extending to the floor of the mouth. The lesion measured approximately 3 cm in greatest dimension. A wide local excision was performed with 1 cm margins around the lesion, including a portion of the anterior floor of the mouth. The surrounding lingual nerve was preserved. The specimen was sent for frozen section, confirming clear margins. Hemostasis was achieved with electrocautery, and a Penrose drain was placed. The patient was extubated and transferred to recovery in stable condition.

Following general anesthesia induction, the patient was intubated and positioned supine. After sterile preparation, a direct laryngoscopy was performed, revealing a large exophytic mass occupying the right vallecula and extending to the base of the tongue. The mass encroached upon the lateral pharyngeal wall and involved the ipsilateral tonsillar fossa. A transoral robotic-assisted resection was undertaken. The mass was excised with wide margins, and frozen section analysis confirmed clear margins. Hemostasis was achieved with bipolar electrocautery. The patient’s vital signs remained stable throughout the procedure, and he was extubated post-operatively with no immediate complications.

The patient was brought to the operating room and anesthesia was induced. After successful intubation, a complete laryngoscopic examination was performed. A diffuse infiltrative lesion was identified involving the left true vocal cord, extending into the anterior commissure and partially affecting the right vocal cord. The tumor was excised using a microdebrider, and CO2 laser was used to achieve hemostasis and ensure clear surgical margins. A frozen section confirmed the absence of tumor at the resection margins. The patient was allowed to awaken from anesthesia without incident and transferred to the recovery unit in stable condition.

Under general anesthesia, the patient was positioned with the head extended for optimal access. A laryngoscope was used to visualize a large infiltrative mass occupying the left piriform sinus, extending into the postcricoid region and involving the arytenoid cartilage. A pharyngotomy was performed to gain access, and the tumor was resected en bloc with negative margins, confirmed by intraoperative frozen section. Due to extensive involvement, a partial laryngectomy was necessary to achieve complete resection. Hemostasis was maintained using hemostatic clips and bipolar cautery. The patient was transferred to the ICU post-operatively for close monitoring.

The procedure begins with the induction of anesthesia and careful intubation. Examination of the oral cavity and pharynx reveals an ulcerative mass on the lateral border of the tongue extending towards the base. Using a mouth retractor, a partial glossectomy is performed. The tumor is excised with a 1 cm margin, involving resection of the tongue’s lateral portion and including a portion of the floor of the mouth where infiltration was noted. Margins are sent for frozen section, and all are confirmed free of tumor. Hemostasis is achieved, and a drain is placed in the floor of the mouth to prevent hematoma formation. The patient is extubated without complications and transferred to recovery.

Following general anesthesia and intubation, the operative field is prepared and draped. Examination with a laryngoscope shows a bulky exophytic tumor involving the right aryepiglottic fold and extending to the lateral wall of the hypopharynx. The tumor is resected using CO2 laser ablation. Careful dissection reveals tumor infiltration into the postcricoid area and right vocal cord. Resection continues with margins sent for frozen section, confirming tumor-free edges. Reconstruction of the aryepiglottic fold is performed using local tissue flaps. The procedure concludes with hemostasis and placement of a nasogastric tube for postoperative nutrition. The patient is extubated and monitored in the ICU postoperatively.

Under general anesthesia, exploration of the oropharyngeal region is carried out using a rigid endoscope. A large fungating mass is identified occupying the entire left tonsillar region, extending into the lateral pharyngeal wall and infiltrating the soft palate. The tumor is excised en bloc, with additional resection of the soft palate to ensure clear margins. The excised tissue is sent for frozen section analysis, revealing clear margins. Reconstruction of the soft palate defect is performed using a local rotational flap. Hemostasis is achieved, and a nasopharyngeal airway is placed to ensure patency. The patient is extubated and transferred to the recovery area in stable condition.

Anesthesia is induced, and the patient is intubated. A detailed examination with a laryngoscope reveals a broad-based tumor at the glottic level, affecting the left vocal cord and extending into the subglottic region. The tumor involves the anterior commissure without crossing to the contralateral side. The laser microsurgery technique is used to resect the tumor, with careful attention to preserving the contralateral vocal cord. After resection, frozen section confirms clear margins. A temporary tracheostomy is performed to ensure airway security postoperatively. The patient is transferred to the recovery unit with a plan for decannulation once laryngeal edema resolves.

Following induction of anesthesia and intubation, the surgical field is exposed using a mouth gag. A large ulcerative lesion is noted at the base of the tongue, extending into the vallecula and involving the adjacent lingual tonsil. The tumor is excised with adequate margins using electrocautery, with care taken to preserve the surrounding vital structures. Frozen section confirms negative margins. Due to the extent of resection, a radial forearm free flap is harvested and used to reconstruct the defect at the tongue base. The flap is anastomosed with microvascular techniques, ensuring adequate perfusion. Hemostasis is achieved, and the patient is awakened from anesthesia and monitored in the ICU for flap viability.

The patient was prepped and draped in the usual manner after induction of anesthesia. A transoral approach was taken to inspect the oropharynx where an exophytic lesion was noted in the right tonsillar fossa extending to the soft palate. A biopsy was taken and sent for frozen section, which confirmed squamous cell carcinoma. Resection was carried out with a wide margin, ensuring clear margins at the soft palate and the base of the tongue. Hemostasis was achieved using bipolar cautery. The defect was reconstructed using a radial forearm free flap. The patient tolerated the procedure well and was extubated without complications.

Under general anesthesia, the patient was positioned supine with neck extension. A direct laryngoscopy was performed, revealing a large, irregular mass on the right true vocal cord, extending into the aryepiglottic fold. The left vocal cord appeared mobile, though slightly erythematous. A CO2 laser was used to meticulously resect the tumor while preserving the contralateral structures. Hemostasis was achieved via topical application of adrenaline. Post-resection, a nasogastric tube was placed for postoperative feeding. The patient was extubated and transferred to recovery in stable condition.

Following induction of anesthesia, the oral cavity was accessed using a retractor. Examination revealed an ulcerated lesion on the lateral border of the left tongue, extending to the floor of the mouth. A wide local excision was performed, including partial glossectomy and resection of the adjacent floor of the mouth tissue. Care was taken to preserve the lingual nerve. The excised tissue was sent for frozen section, confirming clear margins. A primary closure was achieved using absorbable sutures. The patient was extubated and transferred to the recovery room in stable condition.

The patient was brought to the operating room and placed under general anesthesia. A detailed endoscopic examination revealed a fungating mass in the hypopharynx, specifically occupying the left piriform sinus and extending into the postcricoid region. The lesion was noted to cause significant narrowing of the airway. An anterior neck incision was made, and a total laryngopharyngectomy was performed, including resection of the affected piriform sinus. Reconstruction was achieved using a pectoralis major myocutaneous flap. The patient was placed on a ventilator postoperatively and transported to the ICU for monitoring.

Anesthesia was induced, and the patient was positioned with the neck extended. A transoral approach was utilized, and a laryngoscope was introduced to visualize a tumor occupying the anterior two-thirds of the left vocal cord, with mild extension to the anterior commissure. The lesion was excised using a microdebrider, ensuring complete resection of the tumor with preservation of vocal cord function. Pathological examination confirmed the lesion as squamous cell carcinoma with clear margins. The patient was extubated and monitored in the post-anesthesia care unit before being transferred to a regular ward.

After induction of anesthesia and securing the airway via endotracheal intubation, the patient was positioned supine with neck extension. The surgical field was prepped and draped in a sterile fashion. Examination with a direct laryngoscope revealed a large, ulcerative lesion occupying the right lateral oropharyngeal wall, extending into the posterior aspect of the soft palate. The lesion was infiltrative, with poorly defined borders and visible involvement of the tonsillar pillar on the same side. Using electrocautery, we proceeded with a wide local excision of the mass with a 1 cm margin, extending into the base of the tongue and soft palate. Hemostasis was achieved with bipolar cautery. The specimen was sent for frozen section, which confirmed clear margins. The defect was closed primarily with interrupted absorbable sutures. The patient tolerated the procedure well and was extubated without complications.

General anesthesia was induced, and the patient was intubated with a reinforced endotracheal tube. A direct laryngoscopy was performed, which revealed a bulky exophytic tumor on the left vocal fold, extending to the anterior commissure and infiltrating into the subglottic space. The right vocal fold appeared normal. A CO2 laser was used to excise the tumor with preservation of the contralateral vocal fold. The excision extended into the subglottic region where the tumor was ablated in a piecemeal fashion. The resected specimen was sent for intraoperative pathology consultation, which confirmed squamous cell carcinoma with clear resection margins. Post-operative laryngeal edema was minimal, and the patient was extubated successfully.

The patient was brought to the operating room, and following induction of general anesthesia, a complete oro-pharyngo-laryngoscopy was conducted. The inspection revealed an irregular mass at the base of the tongue on the right side, with involvement of the lateral pharyngeal wall. The mass extended posteriorly to the vallecula and superiorly towards the soft palate. A transoral robotic-assisted resection was performed, ensuring a 1 cm margin around the visible tumor mass. The right lingual tonsil was also resected due to suspicious appearance. Hemostasis was secured, and the specimens were sent for histopathological analysis. The procedure was uneventful, and the patient was transferred to the recovery room in stable condition.

Under general anesthesia, the patient was positioned supine, and the neck was extended. A thorough examination with a rigid esophagoscope revealed an extensive tumor originating from the postcricoid region, extending superiorly to involve the entire left pyriform sinus and partially encroaching upon the left aryepiglottic fold. The tumor was also seen infiltrating the upper esophageal sphincter. A transcervical approach was adopted to facilitate complete resection of the mass. The tumor was excised en bloc with clear margins, and tracheostomy was performed to secure the airway post-operatively. The surgical specimen was sent for pathological evaluation, and the results confirmed squamous cell carcinoma.

Following induction of anesthesia, a meticulous examination of the oral cavity was conducted. The inspection revealed an ulcerative lesion on the lateral border of the right tongue, approximately 2 cm in diameter, with extension to the floor of the mouth. The lesion's borders were irregular, and it bled easily upon manipulation. A wide local excision was performed, extending to the floor of the mouth and involving the removal of a segment of the submandibular duct. A frozen section confirmed negative margins. Reconstruction of the defect was carried out using a local mucosal flap. Hemostasis was confirmed, and the patient was extubated and transferred to the recovery room in a stable condition.

Induction of anesthesia followed by nasotracheal intubation. Examination of the oral cavity reveals a large ulcerative lesion on the left lateral border of the tongue extending to the ventral surface. The lesion appears exophytic with irregular borders. A decision is made to proceed with a partial glossectomy. An incision is made along the lesion, ensuring a safety margin of 1 cm. Careful dissection is carried out to preserve the lingual nerve. The excised tissue is sent for frozen section analysis, which confirms clear margins. Hemostasis is achieved, and the wound is closed with absorbable sutures. The patient is extubated after a smooth procedure and transferred to recovery.

General anesthesia is induced, and the patient is intubated. A pharyngolaryngoscopy is performed using a 0° endoscope. Inspection reveals an infiltrative mass occupying the right piriform sinus, extending superiorly to the hypopharyngeal wall. The mass encroaches upon the aryepiglottic fold and the right vocal fold. A transoral laser microsurgery approach is employed. The tumor is resected using a CO2 laser, ensuring adequate margins. The resected tissue is sent for pathological assessment. Hemostasis is secured with bipolar electrocautery. Postoperative laryngeal assessment shows good vocal fold mobility. The patient is extubated and transferred to recovery.

The patient is under general anesthesia with endotracheal intubation. Examination of the oropharynx reveals a fungating mass involving the soft palate and lateral oropharyngeal wall. The tumor extends to the posterior pillar and partially involves the uvula. A transoral robotic-assisted surgical approach is chosen. The mass is excised with robotic instruments, ensuring a 2 cm margin. The uvula is partially resected, and the defect is reconstructed using a radial forearm free flap. The specimen is sent for histopathological analysis. The patient tolerates the procedure well and is extubated in the operating room.

After induction of general anesthesia and nasotracheal intubation, a thorough laryngoscopy is performed. An exophytic lesion is identified on the left vocal fold, extending to the anterior commissure. The lesion also involves the false vocal fold and appears to invade the subglottic region. A direct microlaryngoscopic approach is utilized for resection. The lesion is excised using a cold steel technique, followed by laser ablation for hemostasis and tissue vaporization. The specimen is sent for histological examination. Post-resection, the vocal fold mobility is assessed and found satisfactory. The patient is extubated without complications and transferred to the recovery unit.

The patient is placed under general anesthesia, and orotracheal intubation is performed. Examination of the hypopharynx reveals a large, ulcerative carcinoma involving the posterior pharyngeal wall and extending laterally into the left pyriform sinus. The lesion encroaches upon the laryngeal inlet and the postcricoid region. A laryngopharyngectomy is planned, beginning with an apron flap incision. The larynx and pharynx are mobilized en bloc, and the tumor is excised with wide margins. Reconstruction is performed using a jejunal free flap. The resected specimen is sent for pathologic evaluation. The patient is monitored postoperatively in the ICU.

Anesthesia was induced, and the patient was intubated without complications. Using a mouth gag, the oral cavity was accessed, revealing an ulcerative lesion on the left lateral border of the tongue. A thorough examination demonstrated that the lesion extended posteriorly to the base of the tongue, involving the posterior third. The decision was made to proceed with a partial glossectomy. The lesion was excised with a 2 cm margin, extending into the floor of the mouth. The specimen was sent for frozen section analysis, confirming clear margins. The wound was closed in layers, and the patient was extubated without difficulty.

Following induction of anesthesia and intubation, the patient was positioned for a direct laryngoscopy. Examination revealed a proliferative mass on the anterior commissure and the right vocal fold, extending into the subglottic region. A laser was employed to resect the tumor, ensuring clear margins. The resection was carried out meticulously, with care taken not to damage the contralateral vocal fold. Hemostasis was achieved, and the patient tolerated the procedure well, emerging from anesthesia without complications.

After successful induction of anesthesia and intubation, the oral cavity was accessed using a mouth retractor. Inspection of the oropharynx revealed a large exophytic mass on the left tonsillar fossa extending to the soft palate. The lesion was noted to infiltrate the lateral pharyngeal wall. A wide local excision was performed, with careful dissection to preserve surrounding structures. The excised tissue was sent for pathological evaluation, confirming clear margins. Closure was achieved, and the patient was stable upon transfer to recovery.

Anesthesia was induced, and a comprehensive laryngopharyngoscopy was performed. A large mass was identified in the left piriform sinus, extending to the postcricoid region and involving the aryepiglottic fold. The tumor did not cross the midline. The decision was made for a partial laryngopharyngectomy. The tumor was excised with a 1.5 cm margin, ensuring all involved tissue was removed. Reconstruction of the pharyngeal wall was performed using local tissue flaps. The patient was extubated in the operating room and transferred to the ICU for monitoring.

Induction of anesthesia and oral intubation were performed. Using a laryngoscope, a detailed inspection of the larynx revealed a sessile lesion occupying the entire left vocal fold, encroaching upon the anterior commissure. The lesion was excised using a CO2 laser, with great care to preserve the underlying muscular structure. The surgical field was irrigated and inspected to ensure hemostasis and clear resection margins. The patient was extubated smoothly and transferred to the recovery room in stable condition.

Anesthesia was induced and the patient was intubated by the anesthesiology team. The patient's head was positioned, and a mouth retractor was applied. Examination commenced with a laryngoscope, revealing a fungating mass on the right lateral wall of the hypopharynx, extending to involve the posterior wall. The lesion encroaches upon the postcricoid area. A biopsy was taken from the mass and sent for frozen section analysis, which confirmed squamous cell carcinoma. Resection was performed with a 1.5 cm margin, ensuring the superior and inferior boundaries were clear of tumor infiltration. The surgery concluded with a modified radical neck dissection to address potential lymphatic spread.

Following the induction of anesthesia, the patient was positioned supine with neck extension. An incision was made along the anterior border of the sternocleidomastoid muscle to expose the underlying structures. A tumor involving the left supraglottic larynx was identified, extending to the adjacent glottis but sparing the subglottis. The mass was resected using a CO2 laser, with particular attention paid to preserving the contralateral vocal fold. The excised tissue was sent for a frozen section, which confirmed clear margins. The procedure was completed with a comprehensive neck dissection to assess potential metastatic spread.

The patient was anesthetized and placed in the appropriate surgical position. The oral cavity was accessed via a mouth retractor. A prominent neoplasm was visualized at the posterior third of the tongue, infiltrating the base and extending towards the oropharynx. The tumor was resected with clear margins, extending to involve partial removal of the ipsilateral tonsil tissue. Hemostasis was achieved using electrocautery. A segment of the floor of the mouth was also excised due to tumor infiltration, and the specimen was sent for pathological evaluation. The operation concluded with a tracheostomy to secure the airway.

Anesthesia was initiated, and the patient was prepared for surgery. A comprehensive examination under direct laryngoscopy revealed a large exophytic mass occupying the left pyriform sinus, extending into the postcricoid area and partially obstructing the esophageal inlet. The tumor was excised using a combination of sharp dissection and electrocautery, ensuring a radical resection with clear margins. Intraoperative frozen sections confirmed the absence of tumor cells at the resection margins. The procedure was completed with a primary closure of the mucosal defect and a selective neck dissection to mitigate potential lymphatic spread.

The patient underwent general anesthesia and was positioned for optimal surgical access. Initial inspection with a laryngoscope identified a sizable ulcerative lesion at the anterior two-thirds of the right vocal fold, extending to the anterior commissure and partially onto the left vocal fold. The lesion was excised with a CO2 laser, ensuring precise removal while preserving the structural integrity of the surrounding tissues. Histopathological analysis confirmed clear margins. The surgery proceeded with a microflap technique to reconstruct the resected area, aiming to optimize postoperative phonatory function.

The patient was brought into the operating room and anesthesia was induced, followed by endotracheal intubation. Examination of the oropharynx revealed a large ulcerative lesion on the right lateral wall of the oropharynx extending towards the soft palate and the base of the tongue. A biopsy was performed, confirming a squamous cell carcinoma. A wide local excision of the tumor was carried out, ensuring clear margins. The resection included a portion of the soft palate and the posterior third of the tongue base. Reconstruction was performed using a radial forearm free flap. Hemostasis was achieved, and the patient was extubated postoperatively in a stable condition.

Induction of anesthesia was performed without complications. A direct laryngoscopy was conducted, revealing a large tumor in the supraglottic larynx, extending from the epiglottis across the aryepiglottic folds and into the false vocal cords. The tumor appeared to be obstructing the airway partially. The decision was made to perform a supraglottic laryngectomy. The tumor was excised with a 1 cm margin, ensuring no invasion into the true vocal cords. Reconstruction was done using a local tissue flap, and the airway was secured with a tracheostomy. The patient tolerated the procedure well and was sent to recovery in stable condition.

After induction of anesthesia and intubation, a thorough examination of the oral cavity and oropharynx was performed. An exophytic mass was identified on the left lateral border of the tongue, extending anteriorly towards the tip and posteriorly involving the floor of the mouth. A wide local excision of the lesion was undertaken with a sufficient safety margin. The resection also included part of the floor of the mouth and the sublingual gland. Frozen sections confirmed clear margins. The defect was closed primarily without tension, and meticulous hemostasis was achieved. The patient was extubated and transferred to the recovery area in stable condition.

The patient was prepped and draped in the usual sterile fashion after induction of general anesthesia. Examination with the laryngoscope revealed a large, infiltrative mass occupying the left piriform sinus, extending into the postcricoid area and partially obstructing the esophageal inlet. The tumor also involved the arytenoid cartilage on the same side. A pharyngolaryngectomy was performed, with excision of the tumor en bloc, including part of the esophageal entrance. Reconstruction was achieved using a pectoralis major myocutaneous flap. A tracheostomy was performed to secure the airway. The surgery concluded without complications, and the patient was stable upon transfer to the ICU.

Anesthesia was induced, and the patient was intubated uneventfully. The surgical examination revealed a mass on the right vocal cord, spanning its entire length, and extending into the anterior commissure. The mass also involved the subglottic region slightly. A microlaryngoscopic laser excision was performed, carefully resecting the tumor with a CO2 laser. The anterior commissure and the anterior third of the left vocal cord were involved, requiring partial resection. The subglottic extension was also addressed, ensuring complete removal. The patient was extubated postoperatively and monitored closely in the recovery room.

The patient was anesthetized, and the surgical team proceeded with the induction of general anesthesia. After the patient was intubated, the oral cavity was accessed using a retractor. Examination revealed a large ulcerative lesion located on the lateral border of the tongue, extending toward the base of the tongue and infiltrating the floor of the mouth on the right side. The lesion appeared highly vascular. Resection was performed with a margin of 1 cm around the lesion, involving partial glossectomy and resection of the floor of the mouth. The lingual nerve was preserved, but careful dissection around the hypoglossal nerve was required due to tumor proximity. Hemostasis was achieved, and the specimen was sent for pathological evaluation. An R0 resection was confirmed intraoperatively.

Following induction of anesthesia, direct laryngoscopy was performed, revealing a substantial exophytic mass on the right aryepiglottic fold, extending into the lateral wall of the hypopharynx and involving the piriform sinus. The anterior commissure was clear. Using a CO2 laser, the mass was excised with a focus on preserving the structural integrity of the laryngeal framework. The tumor extended into the muscular layer, necessitating partial resection of the underlying muscle tissue. Frozen section analysis of the margins indicated negative results. Reconstruction of the laryngeal framework was performed using local tissue advancement flaps to optimize functional outcomes.

After induction of anesthesia and intubation, examination under anesthesia was performed using a rigid endoscope. A tumor mass was identified on the left vocal fold, occupying almost the entire fold and extending posteriorly to the arytenoid region. The mass was obstructive, and there was reduced mobility of the affected vocal fold. The decision was made to proceed with laser resection. The entire affected vocal fold was excised, paying careful attention to preserve the contralateral side. The margins were confirmed to be clear on frozen section analysis. Post-operative assessment ensured airway patency was maintained, and the patient was extubated uneventfully.

With the patient under general anesthesia, a thorough examination of the oropharynx was conducted using a pharyngoscope. A large, irregular mass was identified in the tonsillar region on the left side, extending into the soft palate and posteriorly into the lateral pharyngeal wall. The mass was partially obstructing the oropharyngeal passage. Surgical excision of the tumor was achieved using electrocautery, with a 1.5 cm margin around the visible tumor. The surgical field was cleared, and a primary closure was performed to reconstruct the affected oropharyngeal structures. Intraoperative frozen sections confirmed clear resection margins. The patient was transferred to recovery in stable condition.

Induction of anesthesia was followed by intubation and preparation for extensive neck dissection due to a large tumor involving the supraglottic larynx. The mass was found to infiltrate the epiglottis, aryepiglottic folds, and extend into the base of the tongue. A vertical partial laryngectomy was performed, including resection of the epiglottis and the upper half of the thyroid cartilage. Care was taken to preserve the contralateral structures. Reconstruction was carried out using a radial forearm free flap to restore the mucosal defect and maintain functional outcomes. The margins were confirmed to be free of tumor on frozen section analysis. The patient was monitored in the ICU post-operatively.

The patient was brought into the operating room and anesthesia was successfully induced. The initial step involved the placement of a mouth retractor, allowing for optimal visualization of the oropharyngeal region. Examination revealed an ulcerative lesion at the base of the tongue extending to the palatine tonsil on the left side. The lesion was biopsied and confirmed to be malignant. A wide local excision of the tumor was performed, ensuring a safety margin of at least 1 cm surrounding the tumor. The resection extended from the base of the tongue to the soft palate, carefully preserving the lingual nerve. Hemostasis was achieved using bipolar cautery, and the surgical field was irrigated with saline. The specimen was sent for pathology evaluation, and frozen sections confirmed clear margins. The patient was then repositioned for a selective neck dissection to evaluate potential lymphatic spread.

After induction of anesthesia, the patient was positioned supine with slight neck extension. A direct laryngoscopy was performed using a laryngoscope, revealing a fungating mass occupying the right piriform sinus and extending into the aryepiglottic fold. The mass appeared to infiltrate the laryngeal framework, sparing the contralateral structures. A total laryngopharyngectomy was deemed necessary given the extensive involvement of the larynx and pharynx. The incision was made through the skin, and an apron flap was elevated. The larynx was mobilized, preserving the carotid sheath. The tumor was resected en bloc with negative margins, and a tracheostomy was performed to secure the airway. Postoperative care included monitoring for any signs of hemorrhage or infection.

The patient was anesthetized and intubated without complications. A video laryngoscopy revealed a large exophytic lesion on the left vocal cord, causing partial obstruction of the glottic opening. The lesion extended posteriorly to involve the arytenoid cartilage. The decision was made to perform a laser-assisted endoscopic resection. Using a CO2 laser, the lesion was carefully excised with minimal thermal damage to surrounding tissues. Hemostasis was achieved using topical epinephrine. The vocal cord mobility was assessed post-resection, and vocal cord function was preserved. The specimen was sent for histopathological analysis, confirming squamous cell carcinoma. The patient was advised on voice rest and follow-up for further oncological management.

Following induction of general anesthesia, a nasopharyngoscopy was performed, revealing a suspicious lesion in the nasopharynx extending to the oropharynx. The lesion was located at the pharyngeal wall and encroached upon the eustachian tube opening on the left side. A transoral approach was utilized to gain access to the tumor. A careful dissection was performed to isolate the tumor from the surrounding critical structures. The mass was excised completely with clear margins confirmed by intraoperative frozen sections. The surgical field was irrigated, and a nasopharyngeal pack was placed to control bleeding. The patient's recovery was uneventful, and further adjuvant therapy was planned based on the final pathology report.

Anesthesia was induced, and the patient was positioned with the head extended for optimal surgical access. A direct laryngoscopy was performed, revealing a lesion at the glottic level, specifically affecting the anterior two-thirds of the right vocal fold and extending into the subglottic region. The tumor was excised using microdebrider-assisted techniques, ensuring precision and preservation of the surrounding laryngeal structures. The surgical margins were evaluated with frozen sections, confirming complete resection of the carcinoma. Postoperatively, the patient was monitored for airway patency and vocal function. A speech therapy consultation was arranged to assist with postoperative rehabilitation.

Induction of anesthesia by the anesthesia team followed by oral cavity preparation and insertion of the laryngoscope. Examination reveals an exophytic mass involving the anterior two-thirds of the right vocal fold, extending into the anterior commissure but sparing the contralateral side. The subglottic region appears free from tumor involvement. Utilizing a CO2 laser, resection begins at the anterior commissure, continuing laterally to achieve clear margins. Hemostasis is achieved with suction cautery. Pathology confirms complete resection with negative margins. Post-operatively, the patient remains stable and is extubated without complications.

Following the induction of anesthesia, the patient is positioned supine with slight neck extension. A mouth gag is applied to expose the oral cavity. A large ulcerative lesion is noted on the left lateral border of the tongue, extending towards the base. Margins are marked, and resection is performed with a harmonic scalpel, achieving a 2 cm margin. The specimen is sent for frozen section, confirming negative margins. The wound is irrigated and closed with interrupted sutures. The patient tolerates the procedure well, with no immediate post-operative complications.

The patient is anesthetized and intubated, then prepped and draped in the usual sterile fashion. Examination with a laryngoscope reveals a broad-based mass on the right vallecula, extending to the adjacent regions of the epiglottis and the posterior pharyngeal wall. The mass is resected in segments using a CO2 laser, ensuring a 1.5 cm margin into healthy tissue. Care is taken to preserve the integrity of the surrounding structures. Hemostasis is maintained throughout the procedure, and the patient is extubated in the operating room without complications.

Under general anesthesia, the patient is placed in the supine position with head slightly extended. Direct laryngoscopy reveals a fungating mass occupying the hypopharynx, particularly affecting the left piriform sinus and extending to the postcricoid region. The tumor is meticulously resected using a combination of sharp dissection and electrocautery, ensuring clear margins. Anesthesia is induced, and the patient is positioned supine with slight neck extension. The surgical site is sterilized and draped. A large ulcerative lesion is observed at the base of the tongue, extending laterally to involve the left tonsillar fossa. Resection is initiated with a harmonic scalpel following delineation of margins. Dissection proceeds inferiorly to the lingual epiglottic fold, preserving vital structures. The patient is transferred to the intensive care unit for post-operative monitoring.

Following induction of anesthesia and intubation, the patient was positioned supine with slight neck extension. Using a laryngoscope, the oropharynx was visualized, revealing a large, ulcerative lesion on the right side of the soft palate extending into the tonsillar region. The lesion's borders were irregular, and it showed significant infiltration into the surrounding tissue. The lesion was excised with a margin of healthy tissue, employing a cold knife technique. Hemostasis was achieved with bipolar cautery. A portion of the lesion extended into the base of the tongue and was subsequently resected. The excised tissue was sent for frozen section analysis, confirming clear margins. The surgical site was irrigated, and hemostasis was confirmed before closure.

The patient was brought into the operating room, and after successful anesthesia induction, the head was reclined and the mouth opened with a retractor. The tumor was carefully excised with a laser, starting at the vallecula and moving anteriorly to include the anterior surface of the epiglottis. The resection was performed with a margin of healthy tissue, ensuring complete removal. Hemostasis was achieved using a combination of suction and cautery. The site was inspected for any residual tumor tissue before completion.

The procedure began with anesthesia induction and oral intubation, followed by positioning of the patient in a supine position with slight neck extension. A direct laryngoscope was used, revealing a large, fungating tumor on the left vocal fold, extending partially into the anterior commissure. The tumor was excised in sequential segments, and the anterior commissure was carefully inspected for any residual tumor. Hemostasis was maintained with bipolar cautery, and the airway was confirmed to be patent post-resection.

Under general anesthesia, with the patient in a supine position, the oral cavity was accessed using a mouth gag. A mass was noted on the lateral border of the tongue, extending into the floor of the mouth on the left side. The specimen included portions of the tongue and floor of mouth tissue. The wound was irrigated with saline, and bleeding was controlled with electrocautery. A local flap was used to close the defect, and the patient was extubated without complications.

The patient was brought into the operating room and anesthesia was induced smoothly. We proceeded with the placement of the mouth gag and laryngeal exposure using the laryngoscope. Preoperative planning included discussion with the anesthesia team, and general anesthesia was induced successfully. The patient was prepped and draped in the standard fashion after successful induction of general anesthesia. Anesthesia was induced and the patient was placed in a supine position. Under general anesthesia, the patient was positioned supine. A direct laryngoscopy was performed using a Lindholm laryngoscope for optimal visualization. Following induction of anesthesia, a suspension microlaryngoscopy was performed. Examination revealed a fungating mass on the left base of the tongue, infiltrating into the adjacent soft palate. The patient was anesthetized, and a laryngoscopy was performed using a 0° endoscope. Anesthesia was induced, and an examination with a rigid esophagoscope was conducted. The patient was brought to the operating room and anesthesia was induced. After achieving adequate anesthesia, the mouth was opened with a retractor and the surgical field was prepared. Anesthesia was induced, and the patient was intubated without complications. After successful induction of anesthesia and intubation, the patient's neck was prepared for surgery. The patient was anesthetized and intubated, followed by a careful inspection of the upper aerodigestive tract. Under general anesthesia, the patient underwent a thorough examination of the oral cavity and oropharynx. Anesthesia was induced, and the patient was intubated without complications. A mouth gag was applied, and the oral cavity was examined. The patient was brought to the operating room, where anesthesia was induced and intubation performed. Anesthesia was induced and the patient was intubated without complications. The patient was prepped and draped following successful induction of anesthesia. Examination with a flexible endoscope showed a suspicious lesion at the posterior aspect of the left arytenoid and extending into the postcricoid region. A transoral approach was utilized to access the larynx, and the lesion was resected using a laser with adequate margins. The resection included part of the left vocal fold and arytenoid. Frozen section confirmed clear margins. No significant bleeding was observed, and a nasogastric tube was placed for postoperative nutrition.

General anesthesia was administered, and the patient was intubated. Examination with a rigid esophagoscope revealed a substantial mass at the posterior pharyngeal wall, extending into the oropharynx and partially involving the lateral pharyngeal wall. The mass was biopsied, and a transoral laser microsurgery was performed, resecting the tumor with 1 cm margins. The tumor was noted to invade the upper esophageal sphincter, necessitating partial esophagectomy. Frozen section analysis was consistent with tumor-free margins. The patient was transferred to the ICU for observation.

The procedure began with the induction of general anesthesia and intubation. Upon visual inspection using a laryngoscope, a polypoid mass was identified on the anterior two-thirds of the right vocal fold, with involvement of the anterior commissure. The lesion was excised using cold steel instruments, followed by laser ablation to achieve hemostasis and ensure complete removal. The resection was carried out with care to preserve the function of the remaining vocal fold. Postoperative examination confirmed no residual tumor. The patient was extubated and transferred to recovery in stable condition.

The patient was prepped for surgery, and anesthesia was induced without complications. Examination with a laryngoscope revealed an ulcerated lesion on the posterior third of the left vocal fold, extending into the arytenoid region. The lesion appeared to infiltrate the left ventricle. The right vocal fold and subglottic area were clear. A CO2 laser was used for precise excision of the lesion, with careful preservation of surrounding structures. The specimen was sent for pathological evaluation. Hemostasis was achieved, and the patient was extubated and monitored in recovery.

Anesthesia was smoothly induced, followed by the insertion of the laryngoscope. A large, irregular mass was identified on the right lateral wall of the oropharynx, involving the tonsillar region and extending into the base of the tongue. The mass also encroached upon the posterior pharyngeal wall. Surgical excision involved a wide local resection to ensure clear margins, including partial resection of the base of the tongue and posterior pharyngeal wall. Frozen sections confirmed negative margins. Reconstruction was performed using a radial forearm free flap for defect coverage.

Following induction of anesthesia, a direct laryngoscopy was performed, which revealed a fungating mass occupying the entire right piriform sinus. The tumor extended inferiorly to the esophageal inlet and superiorly to the aryepiglottic fold. The laryngeal framework was carefully preserved during the resection process. A total laryngopharyngectomy was performed due to extensive infiltration, and a primary tracheoesophageal puncture was created for voice rehabilitation. A pectoralis major myocutaneous flap was utilized for reconstruction.

The patient was intubated without difficulty, and the surgical field was prepared. Examination revealed a small lesion on the ventral surface of the tongue, approximately 1.2 cm in diameter, located at the junction of the anterior two-thirds and posterior third. A carbon dioxide laser was used for excision, with a 5 mm margin to ensure complete resection. The submucosa was also included in the resection to achieve clear margins. The defect was closed primarily, and hemostasis was achieved with bipolar cautery.

The patient underwent general anesthesia and was positioned supine. A laryngoscope examination showed a large exophytic tumor on the left supraglottic larynx, involving the epiglottis and extending to the false vocal cord. A partial supraglottic laryngectomy was performed, with resection of the epiglottis, left aryepiglottic fold, and part of the false vocal cord. Care was taken to preserve the true vocal cords and maintain their function. The specimen was sent for frozen section, confirming that margins were free of tumor. Reconstruction was achieved with a local mucosal advancement flap.

Following induction of anesthesia and oral intubation, the patient was positioned supine with adequate neck extension. A direct laryngoscopy was performed using a rigid laryngoscope, revealing a large, ulcerative lesion occupying the right vallecula and extending to the lateral pharyngeal wall. The lesion was biopsied for histopathological evaluation, and a transoral laser microsurgery was initiated to excise the tumor. The excision was performed with a 1.5 cm margin, ensuring clear margins on frozen section analysis. Reconstruction was achieved using a radial forearm free flap to restore the defect in the oropharyngeal region, with microvascular anastomosis confirming adequate perfusion post-reconstruction. The patient tolerated the procedure well, with no intraoperative complications.

General anesthesia was induced, and the patient was intubated nasotracheally. A direct laryngoscopy was performed, revealing an exophytic mass on the anterior two-thirds of the left vocal cord with partial obstruction of the airway. The tumor did not cross the midline but extended to involve the anterior commissure. A CO2 laser was utilized to perform a cordectomy, meticulously preserving the right vocal cord to maintain phonatory function. Hemostasis was achieved using bipolar cautery. Post-operatively, the patient was extubated without difficulty and was observed in the recovery room with stable vital signs.

Under general anesthesia, the patient was positioned, and a wide exposure of the oral cavity was achieved using a mouth gag. Inspection revealed a large ulcerative lesion on the lateral border of the tongue, extending to the floor of the mouth. Marginal mandibulectomy was performed to achieve adequate surgical margins, and a selective neck dissection was carried out to address potential lymphatic spread. The primary defect was closed using a local advancement flap. Histopathology confirmed clear margins. The patient was transferred to the recovery unit in stable condition, with plans for postoperative radiotherapy.

Following successful induction of general anesthesia and nasal intubation, a comprehensive examination was conducted using a 70-degree endoscope. A fungating tumor was identified in the postcricoid region with extension into the hypopharynx and partial involvement of the arytenoid cartilage. A combined open and endoscopic approach was employed for resection. The tumor was excised en bloc with a margin, and a partial pharyngectomy was performed. Reconstruction involved a pectoralis major flap to restore the defect. The patient was monitored closely in the ICU post-operatively, with a nasogastric tube placed for enteral nutrition.

The patient underwent induction of anesthesia and was intubated with a reinforced endotracheal tube. A rigid esophagoscope was introduced, and a thorough examination was performed, revealing an infiltrative lesion at the base of the tongue extending to the lingual surface of the epiglottis. The lesion was resected using a transoral robotic approach, ensuring preservation of critical neurovascular structures. Intraoperative frozen sections confirmed negative margins. Reconstruction was achieved with a split-thickness skin graft. The procedure was completed without complications, and the patient was extubated and transferred to the surgical ward for observation.

After the anesthesiologist has induced anesthesia and secured the airway, the patient is positioned supine. A mouth retractor is placed, and the tongue is retracted for better visualization. Examination reveals an ulcerated lesion located on the right tonsillar fossa extending to the lateral pharyngeal wall. The lesion is exophytic with significant infiltration into the adjacent soft tissues. A wide local excision is performed, ensuring a margin of at least 1 cm on all sides. The resection includes part of the soft palate and the upper section of the lateral pharyngeal wall. The tissue is sent for frozen section analysis, confirming clear margins. Hemostasis is achieved, and closure is performed in layers. The patient tolerated the procedure well with no immediate complications.

Induction of anesthesia and endotracheal intubation were performed by the anesthesia team. With the patient in the supine position, direct laryngoscopy was conducted using a laryngoscope. Inspection revealed a large mass involving the left true vocal cord, extending to the anterior commissure and partially involving the right vocal cord. The tumor was causing significant narrowing of the airway. Laser microsurgery was employed to excise the tumor, starting from the anterior commissure and progressing laterally to preserve as much of the vocal cord structure as possible. The excised tissue was sent for histopathological evaluation. The airway was clear post-resection, and the patient was extubated successfully in the operating room.

Anesthesia was induced, and the patient was positioned supine with neck extension. The oral cavity was opened using a mouth gag. Examination revealed a prominent mass on the left lateral border of the tongue, extending posteriorly to involve the base of the tongue and crossing the midline. The decision was made for a partial glossectomy. The resection included the affected portion of the tongue, extending into the floor of the mouth. Margins were checked intraoperatively and confirmed to be free of tumor. Additional dissection was carried out to remove involved submandibular lymph nodes. Hemostasis was secured, and reconstruction involved primary closure with local flaps. The patient was extubated postoperatively and transferred to recovery in stable condition.

Following induction of anesthesia, a comprehensive examination of the larynx was conducted using a rigid endoscope. There was a papillomatous growth observed on the supraglottic larynx, specifically on the epiglottis, extending to the aryepiglottic fold bilaterally. The subglottic region and trachea appeared clear. Using a CO2 laser, the mass was carefully excised while preserving laryngeal function. Frozen section confirmed a benign papillomatosis without malignancy. The patient was monitored for airway patency throughout the procedure and was extubated without complication. The postoperative course was unremarkable.

Anesthesia was induced, and the patient was intubated. Performing a panendoscopy, a large, ulcerative lesion was identified in the hypopharynx, predominantly affecting the postcricoid region and extending to the cervical esophagus. The tumor had invaded the left piriform sinus and spread towards the arytenoid region. A wide-field resection was planned, and the tumor was excised en bloc with surrounding tissue to ensure clear margins. The resection extended to involve the lateral pharyngeal wall and included a partial resection of the arytenoid cartilages. Following resection, a radial forearm free flap was utilized for reconstruction to restore continuity of the hypopharyngeal wall. The patient was transferred to the intensive care unit for postoperative monitoring.

Anesthesia is induced and intubation performed by anesthesiology colleagues. After sterile preparation, the surgical team begins with an inspection using the laryngoscope. A large ulcerating mass is observed on the medial surface of the right tonsil, extending posteriorly to involve the posterior pharyngeal wall. The tumor appears to infiltrate the soft palate as well. A wide local excision is performed, including the right tonsil and involved portions of the soft palate and pharyngeal wall. Frozen sections are sent to confirm margins. Reconstruction is achieved using a radial forearm free flap to cover the defect in the pharyngeal wall and soft palate. The procedure concludes with careful hemostasis and placement of a nasogastric feeding tube.

Under general anesthesia, the patient is positioned, and a direct laryngoscopy is performed with the aid of a microscope. A large exophytic lesion is seen arising from the anterior two-thirds of the left vocal fold, with extension into the anterior commissure and involvement of the subglottic region. The lesion is excised using CO2 laser, maintaining a safety margin of healthy tissue. The resection includes the anterior portion of the left vocal fold, sparing the posterior third. Hemostasis is achieved, and the specimen is sent for histopathological examination. The airway is clear post-procedure, and the patient is extubated without complications.

Following induction of anesthesia, the oral cavity is accessed using a Dingman retractor. A fungating tumor occupying the posterior third of the tongue on the right side is observed, extending to the lateral floor of the mouth. Careful dissection is performed to resect the tumor with a 2 cm margin, involving partial glossectomy and floor of mouth resection. The lingual nerve on the right is preserved, and a segment of the sublingual gland is removed. The surgical bed is irrigated and checked for hemostasis. Closure is achieved using interrupted sutures, and a drain is placed in the floor of the mouth.

The patient is placed under general anesthesia, and a tracheostomy is performed to secure the airway. Examination reveals a large, infiltrative mass in the supraglottic larynx, involving the epiglottis and aryepiglottic folds, with extension into the right piriform sinus. The tumor is excised through a lateral pharyngotomy approach, including resection of the right arytenoid cartilage and partial resection of the epiglottis. The right piriform sinus is also cleared of tumor. Reconstruction is achieved using a pectoralis major myocutaneous flap. Hemostasis is confirmed, and a feeding tube is inserted for postoperative nutrition.

With the patient anesthetized and intubated, endoscopic examination of the larynx reveals a firm, nodular mass on the left false vocal cord, extending to the true vocal cord and anterior commissure. The tumor does not cross the midline but causes significant narrowing of the airway. A microlaryngeal surgical approach is employed to excise the mass using a CO2 laser. The resection includes the false and true vocal cords on the left, with a portion of the anterior commissure. Hemostasis is meticulously achieved, and the resected tissue is sent for pathology. Postoperative airway patency is confirmed, and the patient is extubated successfully.

Upon induction of anesthesia and insertion of the endotracheal tube, the patient was positioned supine with the head reclined. A thorough inspection of the oral cavity using a tongue depressor revealed an ulcerative lesion on the left lateral border of the tongue, extending posteriorly to involve parts of the base of the tongue. The lesion was approximately 3 cm in diameter with raised, indurated edges. A wide local excision was performed, ensuring a 1 cm margin of healthy tissue around the lesion. The excised specimen was sent for frozen section, confirming negative margins. The wound was closed primarily, and hemostasis was achieved. The lingual nerve was carefully preserved during the procedure.

Following successful induction of anesthesia, a direct laryngoscopy was performed using a laryngoscope. The examination revealed a large, irregularly shaped mass occupying the supraglottic region and extending into the glottic area. The mass was exophytic and involved both vocal cords, causing partial airway obstruction. A CO2 laser was utilized to debulk the tumor, starting from the anterior commissure and progressing laterally. The tumor was excised in its entirety, with care taken to preserve the arytenoid and cricoid cartilages. Post-operative inspection confirmed clear margins, and the specimen was sent for histopathological evaluation.

The patient was brought into the operating theater and successfully intubated. A flexible nasoendoscope was used to examine the nasopharynx, revealing a small, polypoid lesion at the posterior wall of the oropharynx, extending laterally to involve the posterior pillar of the right tonsil. A biopsy was taken, confirming squamous cell carcinoma. The decision was made to perform a transoral robotic surgery (TORS) for resection. The robotic arms were positioned, and the lesion was excised with a 1.5 cm margin. The resection was carried out meticulously, preserving the surrounding healthy structures. Hemostasis was achieved, and the specimen was sent for frozen section analysis, confirming complete excision with negative margins.

After induction of anesthesia, a thorough examination of the hypopharynx was conducted using a rigid esophagoscope. A large ulcerative mass was identified in the lower part of the pharynx, extending into the proximal esophagus and infiltrating the piriform sinus on the left side. The mass was friable and bled easily upon contact. A transoral approach was utilized for resection, starting at the proximal edge of the tumor and progressing inferiorly. The resection was extended laterally to include the involved piriform sinus. Intraoperative frozen sections confirmed clean margins. Reconstruction of the pharyngeal defect was accomplished using a radial forearm free flap.

Following anesthesia induction, a comprehensive endoscopic evaluation was performed using a laryngoscope. An extensive exophytic lesion was found occupying the entire right piriform sinus, with extension into the postcricoid area. The lesion was biopsied previously and confirmed as squamous cell carcinoma. A combined open and endoscopic approach was taken to resect the tumor. An incision was made along the anterior neck, and the larynx was mobilized. The tumor was carefully excised, maintaining a safe margin. The defect was reconstructed using a pectoralis major myocutaneous flap. The procedure was completed with no immediate complications, and the patient was transferred to the recovery unit in stable condition.

Following induction of anesthesia and endotracheal intubation, the patient was positioned supine with slight neck extension. A direct laryngoscopy was performed using a laryngoscope. The examination revealed a large ulcerative mass at the base of the tongue extending posteriorly to the vallecula and partially obstructing the airway. Biopsies were taken for frozen section analysis, confirming squamous cell carcinoma. The tumor was resected with a safety margin, including part of the tongue base and vallecula. Hemostasis was achieved using bipolar cautery. Reconstruction involved local tissue advancement flaps. The patient tolerated the procedure well with no immediate complications.

Anesthesia was induced, and the patient was intubated nasally. The surgical team proceeded with a transoral approach using a laryngoscope for visualization. A large exophytic mass was identified on the left lateral wall of the oropharynx, extending into the tonsillar fossa and invading the soft palate. The mass was carefully excised, and the defect was closed with sutures. Biopsy of the surrounding tissue margins ensured complete resection. The frozen section confirmed negative margins. Postoperative bleeding was minimal, and the patient was extubated successfully in the operating room.

General anesthesia was administered, and the patient was intubated orally. Using a rigid laryngoscope, inspection revealed a tumor occupying the entirety of the right vocal fold, extending into the anterior commissure and subglottic region. The lesion was excised using a CO2 laser, ensuring the preservation of the contralateral vocal fold. The anterior commissure was resected, and the remaining mucosa was cauterized to prevent bleeding. The patient’s postoperative voice was hoarse but intact, and the airway was patent.

Under general anesthesia, the patient underwent a transoral robotic-assisted surgery. Initial examination with the endoscope showed a malignant lesion in the hypopharynx, originating in the right piriform sinus and extending into the postcricoid area. The tumor was resected in full, including part of the aryepiglottic fold to ensure clear margins. The defect was left to heal by secondary intention. Intraoperative frozen sections confirmed a complete resection with clear margins. The patient was transferred to recovery in stable condition.

With the patient under general anesthesia, a direct laryngoscopy was performed. An exophytic lesion was found on the right side of the supraglottic larynx, extending into the epiglottis and aryepiglottic fold. The lesion was excised in full using a laser, and the surrounding tissue was carefully examined. The excision extended to the pre-epiglottic space to ensure clear margins. Intraoperative pathology confirmed squamous cell carcinoma with negative margins. The patient was extubated and monitored in the PACU with no immediate complications.
